questions.stem: A 46-year-old man presents to his physician after being denied life insurance due to an abnormal ECG showing left ventricular hypertrophy. His blood pressure is 148/86 mmHg. Cardiac auscultation reveals a crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur heard best at the right second intercostal space with radiation to the carotid arteries. Echocardiography confirms a stenotic aortic valve with two leaflets. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of this patient's valve disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Degenerative calcification of a normally formed tricuspid aortic valve

choices.label: B
choices.text: Congenital bicuspid aortic valve with accelerated calcification

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rheumatic heart disease with post-inflammatory valve scarring

choices.label: D
choices.text: Infective endocarditis with valve destruction and vegetations

choices.label: E
choices.text: Radiation-induced valvulopathy from prior mediastinal treatment

explanation.summary: A 46-year-old with aortic stenosis and an echocardiogram showing two leaflets has a congenital bicuspid aortic valve, the most common congenital malformation of the aortic valve, which develops AS earlier (40s-50s) than degenerative calcification (70s-80s).
explanation.whyCorrect: A bicuspid aortic valve is the most common congenital malformation of the aortic valve (1-2% of the population). The two leaflets cannot withstand normal wear and tear as well and tend to calcify more easily, leading to AS in patients' 40s or 50s rather than the 70s-80s typical of degenerative calcification of a normal tricuspid valve.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Degenerative calcification of a normally formed tricuspid aortic valve typically causes aortic stenosis in patients aged 70-80, not in a 46-year-old, and the echocardiogram shows two leaflets, not three.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rheumatic heart disease can cause aortic valve stenosis from scarring and calcification, but the echocardiogram demonstrating only two leaflets points to a congenital bicuspid valve as the primary etiology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Infective endocarditis causes valve destruction with vegetations and regurgitation rather than the progressive stenosis with calcification seen in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Radiation-induced valvulopathy is rare and typically occurs decades after mediastinal radiation therapy, which is not part of this patient's history.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with known severe aortic stenosis presents to the emergency department with crushing substernal chest pain. His blood pressure is 100/70 mmHg and heart rate is 92 bpm. Which of the following medications should be specifically avoided in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous normal saline for volume resuscitation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sublingual nitroglycerin for chest pain relief

choices.label: C
choices.text: Low-dose aspirin for antiplatelet therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oral acetaminophen for analgesic effect

explanation.summary: Nitroglycerin should be avoided in patients with severe aortic stenosis because it causes venodilation, reducing venous return and preload, which these patients are critically dependent upon to maintain cardiac output.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with severe AS are preload dependent. Nitroglycerin is a vasodilator that reduces venous return and preload. In severe AS, where the LV must generate extremely high pressures to eject blood through the stenotic valve, any reduction in preload can cause rapid hemodynamic decompensation and potentially fatal hypotension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Intravenous normal saline would increase preload, which is beneficial in patients with severe AS who are preload dependent, and should not be avoided.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Low-dose aspirin for antiplatelet therapy does not affect preload or afterload and is not contraindicated in patients with severe aortic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Supplemental oxygen delivery via nasal cannula supports tissue oxygenation and has no adverse hemodynamic effects in patients with severe aortic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oral acetaminophen provides analgesic effect without vasodilatory properties and is safe in patients with severe aortic stenosis.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with severe aortic stenosis is evaluated in the cardiology clinic. She reports progressive dyspnea on exertion and bilateral lower extremity edema over the past 6 weeks. Echocardiography reveals an ejection fraction of 35% and severe concentric left ventricular hypertrophy. Which of the following best describes the sequence of pathophysiological events leading to her current presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased afterload → concentric LV hypertrophy → diastolic dysfunction → systolic dysfunction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased preload → eccentric LV hypertrophy → systolic dysfunction → diastolic dysfunction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mitral regurgitation → LV volume overload → eccentric hypertrophy → systolic dysfunction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Coronary artery occlusion → myocardial infarction → LV remodeling → systolic dysfunction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pulmonary hypertension → right ventricular failure → decreased LV preload → systolic dysfunction

explanation.summary: In aortic stenosis, the increased pressure gradient (afterload) causes concentric LV hypertrophy, which initially causes diastolic dysfunction (nonsystolic HF) and eventually progresses to systolic HF as the muscle weakens.
explanation.whyCorrect: AS increases afterload on the LV, leading to concentric left ventricular hypertrophy as a compensatory mechanism. The thickened ventricle has decreased compliance, causing diastolic dysfunction (nonsystolic HF) first. As the disease progresses, the heart muscle eventually weakens from chronic pressure overload, leading to systolic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Aortic stenosis increases afterload, not decreases preload, as the primary insult. It causes concentric (not eccentric) hypertrophy from pressure overload, and diastolic dysfunction precedes systolic dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mitral regurgitation causes volume overload with eccentric hypertrophy, which is a different pathophysiological process than the pressure overload and concentric hypertrophy seen in aortic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While coronary artery disease shares risk factors with AS, the patient's presentation with severe concentric LV hypertrophy and progressive AS indicates pressure overload as the cause of systolic dysfunction, not acute coronary occlusion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary hypertension and right ventricular failure can be consequences of advanced AS but are not the primary pathophysiological sequence causing the left ventricular systolic dysfunction described.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known moderate aortic stenosis is being evaluated for worsening symptoms. His cardiologist performs a comprehensive echocardiogram with Doppler. Which of the following measurements obtained from this study is most important for determining the severity and guiding treatment decisions for his aortic stenosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Left atrial diameter and volume index

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pressure gradient across the aortic valve and valve area

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mitral valve E/A ratio and deceleration time

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pulmonary artery systolic pressure estimation

explanation.summary: Doppler echocardiography determines the pressure gradient across the stenotic aortic valve and valve area, which are the key measurements for staging severity and guiding treatment in aortic stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Doppler echocardiography measures the direction and velocity of blood flow through the aortic valve, allowing calculation of the pressure gradient between the LV and aorta and the aortic valve area. Both measurements are critical for staging AS severity: a valve area <1 cm² indicates severe stenosis, and the pressure gradient correlates with the degree of obstruction and LV strain.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Left atrial diameter may reflect chronically elevated filling pressures but is not the primary measurement used to determine aortic stenosis severity or guide valve-specific treatment decisions.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Tricuspid annular plane systolic excursion assesses right ventricular function and is not directly relevant to grading aortic stenosis severity or determining treatment approach.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mitral valve E/A ratio and deceleration time assess diastolic filling patterns and diastolic dysfunction, which may be secondary to AS but do not directly determine AS severity or treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary artery pressure estimation assesses pulmonary hemodynamics and may be elevated as a consequence of AS but does not directly determine the severity of the aortic valve stenosis itself.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with a recently diagnosed bicuspid aortic valve undergoes additional imaging workup. His cardiologist explains that patients with bicuspid aortic valves are at risk for a specific aortic complication and should be screened accordingly. Which of the following complications is most closely associated with bicuspid aortic valve and warrants additional screening?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Abdominal aortic aneurysm below the renal arteries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aortic root dilation with risk of dissection or rupture

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coarctation of the descending thoracic aorta

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atherosclerotic plaque formation in the aortic arch

choices.label: E
choices.text: Thrombotic occlusion of the iliac artery bifurcation

explanation.summary: Bicuspid aortic valve is associated with dilation of the aortic root (aortic aneurysm), which puts the aorta at risk of dissection or rupture and requires screening when a bicuspid valve is identified.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bicuspid aortic valve is associated with dilation of the aortic root, termed aortic aneurysm, which is analogous to the stretching of a balloon. This puts the aorta at risk of dissection (tear in the vessel wall lining) or rupture. When a bicuspid valve is identified, the patient should also be screened for aortic root aneurysm.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Abdominal aortic aneurysm below the renal arteries is associated with smoking, advanced age, and male sex, not specifically with bicuspid aortic valve, which primarily affects the aortic root.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While coarctation can coexist with bicuspid aortic valve in some congenital syndromes, the source material specifically identifies aortic root dilation as the complication requiring screening in bicuspid valve patients.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atherosclerotic plaque formation in the aortic arch is related to general cardiovascular risk factors and is not the specific complication associated with bicuspid aortic valve that warrants additional screening.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thrombotic occlusion of the iliac bifurcation is a manifestation of peripheral arterial disease unrelated to the congenital bicuspid aortic valve and its associated aortopathy.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with severe aortic stenosis is being examined. His physician simultaneously auscultates the heart and palpates the carotid arteries. The physician notes that the carotid upstroke is noticeably delayed after the S2 heart sound, and the pulse feels weak. Which of the following terms best describes this physical examination finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulsus paradoxus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulsus alternans

choices.label: C
choices.text: Water hammer pulse

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pulsus parvus et tardus

choices.label: E
choices.text: Pulsus bisferiens

explanation.summary: Pulsus parvus et tardus (parvus = weak, tardus = late) is a delayed, dulled carotid pulse that indicates severe aortic stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pulsus parvus et tardus describes a carotid pulse that is both weak (parvus) and delayed (tardus). In severe AS, it takes longer to complete LV contraction and close the aortic valve, resulting in a noticeably delayed carotid upstroke after the S2 heart sound. This finding indicates severe aortic stenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulsus paradoxus is an exaggerated decrease in systolic blood pressure during inspiration (>10 mmHg) and is associated with cardiac tamponade and severe asthma, not aortic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulsus alternans describes alternating strong and weak pulses with each heartbeat and is a sign of severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction, not specifically of aortic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Water hammer pulse (Corrigan pulse) is a bounding, collapsing pulse associated with aortic regurgitation, the opposite hemodynamic abnormality of aortic stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulsus bisferiens is a double-peaked pulse found in combined aortic stenosis and regurgitation or hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, not in isolated severe aortic stenosis.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with severe aortic stenosis is seen in clinic. She reports that she has been asymptomatic and feels well. Echocardiography confirms severe AS with an aortic valve area of 0.8 cm² and a mean gradient of 48 mmHg. She has no other cardiac conditions and is not planning any surgical procedures. Which of the following is the most appropriate management strategy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate surgical aortic valve replacement given the valve area

choices.label: B
choices.text: Watchful waiting with serial echocardiograms to monitor progression

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiation of nitroglycerin therapy to reduce the pressure gradient

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transcatheter aortic valve replacement within the next two weeks

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aggressive diuretic therapy to reduce left ventricular preload

explanation.summary: Asymptomatic patients with severe AS can be managed with watchful waiting and serial echocardiograms. Valve replacement is indicated when symptoms develop or if the patient requires cardiac surgery for another reason.
explanation.whyCorrect: If a patient with severe AS is asymptomatic, watchful waiting with repeat echocardiograms is recommended to monitor for progression. Aortic valve replacement is not typically indicated until the patient develops symptoms (angina, syncope, dyspnea) or is undergoing cardiac surgery for another reason.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Immediate surgical valve replacement is not indicated for asymptomatic severe AS without other cardiac surgical indications. The patient should be monitored with serial echocardiograms until symptoms develop.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nitroglycerin is specifically contraindicated in patients with severe AS because it reduces preload through venodilation, and these patients are preload dependent to maintain cardiac output.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transcatheter aortic valve replacement is a treatment option for symptomatic severe AS, but this patient is asymptomatic and does not currently require valve replacement.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aggressive diuretic therapy would reduce preload, which is dangerous in patients with severe AS who depend on adequate preload to maintain cardiac output through the stenotic valve.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man with a known bicuspid aortic valve and moderate aortic stenosis is being counseled about his condition. He asks why he developed valve disease at such a young age while his 80-year-old father was recently diagnosed with similar valve disease. Which of the following best explains the difference in age of onset between this patient and his father?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The father likely has rheumatic valve disease from childhood streptococcal infection

choices.label: B
choices.text: The two-leaflet valve is less durable and calcifies earlier than a normal three-leaflet valve

choices.label: C
choices.text: The patient likely has an autoimmune condition accelerating valve inflammation

choices.label: D
choices.text: The father's valve disease is caused by infective endocarditis from a recent bacteremia

choices.label: E
choices.text: The patient has undiagnosed Marfan syndrome causing premature valve degeneration

explanation.summary: A bicuspid aortic valve has two leaflets that cannot withstand normal wear and tear as well as a normal tricuspid valve, leading to earlier calcification and stenosis in the 40s-50s versus the 70s-80s.
explanation.whyCorrect: The two leaflets of the bicuspid valve are not able to hold up as well to the normal wear and tear on the aortic valve and tend to calcify more easily than a normal tricuspid valve. This is why patients with bicuspid valves develop AS in their 40s or 50s, while those with normal (tricuspid) aortic valves develop AS in their 70s or 80s from degenerative calcification.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While rheumatic heart disease can cause aortic stenosis, the question states the father was recently diagnosed at age 80, which is consistent with degenerative calcification of a normal tricuspid valve, not rheumatic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no indication of an autoimmune condition in this patient. The earlier onset of AS is explained by the inherent vulnerability of the bicuspid valve to calcification, not autoimmune inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Infective endocarditis causes acute valve destruction with vegetations and regurgitation rather than the chronic progressive stenosis from calcification described in the father.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While Marfan syndrome can affect the aorta and aortic valve, the patient has a known bicuspid aortic valve, which is the most common congenital valve malformation and fully explains the earlier age of onset.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with recently diagnosed moderate aortic stenosis is evaluated. He has a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, and a 30-pack-year smoking history. His physician explains that his risk factors for aortic valve calcification also place him at elevated risk for which of the following conditions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coronary artery disease with atherosclerotic plaque formation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy with asymmetric septal hypertrophy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dilated cardiomyopathy from viral myocarditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mitral valve prolapse with myxomatous degeneration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Restrictive cardiomyopathy from amyloid deposition

explanation.summary: The risk factors for aortic valve calcification (older age, male sex, smoking, hypertension, high LDL cholesterol, diabetes mellitus) are the same as those for coronary artery disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: The risk factors for aortic valve calcification, including older age, male sex, smoking, hypertension, high LDL levels, and diabetes mellitus, are the same risk factors for coronary artery disease. CAD occurs when plaque builds up in the coronary arteries, potentially causing angina and myocardial infarction.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy is a genetic condition caused by sarcomeric protein mutations, not by the cardiovascular risk factors shared between aortic valve calcification and coronary artery disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dilated cardiomyopathy from viral myocarditis is caused by viral infection and autoimmune myocardial damage, unrelated to the atherosclerotic risk factors for aortic valve calcification.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mitral valve prolapse with myxomatous degeneration is related to connective tissue abnormalities, not to the atherosclerotic risk factors shared between aortic valve calcification and coronary artery disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Restrictive cardiomyopathy from amyloidosis is caused by abnormal protein deposition and is unrelated to the modifiable cardiovascular risk factors for aortic valve calcification.

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with severe aortic stenosis presents with worsening dyspnea on exertion over the past 2 months and an episode of near-syncope last week. His echocardiogram shows an aortic valve area of 0.7 cm² and a mean pressure gradient of 52 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate definitive treatment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Long-term oral anticoagulation with warfarin therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Daily sublingual nitroglycerin for symptom management

choices.label: C
choices.text: Serial echocardiographic monitoring every six months

choices.label: D
choices.text: Initiation of high-dose statin therapy to halt valve calcification

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortic valve replacement via surgical or catheter-based approach

explanation.summary: The only definitive treatment for symptomatic severe aortic stenosis is aortic valve replacement, which can be performed through open-heart surgery or through a minimally invasive catheter-based approach (TAVR).
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient has symptomatic severe AS (valve area 0.7 cm², mean gradient 52 mmHg, with dyspnea and near-syncope). The only definitive treatment for symptomatic severe AS is aortic valve replacement, which can be achieved through open-heart surgery or through a minimally invasive catheter-based procedure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Anticoagulation with warfarin does not treat the mechanical obstruction caused by aortic stenosis and is not indicated as treatment for AS itself.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Nitroglycerin is specifically contraindicated in severe AS because it reduces preload through venodilation, which can cause dangerous hemodynamic decompensation in preload-dependent patients.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Serial echocardiographic monitoring is appropriate for asymptomatic severe AS, but this patient is symptomatic with dyspnea and near-syncope, requiring definitive intervention rather than continued observation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While statin therapy addresses cardiovascular risk factors, it has not been shown to halt or reverse aortic valve calcification and does not address the mechanical obstruction requiring definitive valve replacement.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old man with severe aortic stenosis is evaluated in the cardiology clinic. During cardiac auscultation, the physician asks the patient to perform a Valsalva maneuver. Which of the following changes in the aortic stenosis murmur would be expected during this maneuver?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The murmur becomes louder due to increased turbulent flow across the valve

choices.label: B
choices.text: The murmur becomes quieter due to decreased blood flow through the aortic valve

choices.label: C
choices.text: The murmur changes from systolic to diastolic timing

choices.label: D
choices.text: The murmur radiates more prominently to the cardiac apex

choices.label: E
choices.text: The murmur develops a continuous machinery-like quality

explanation.summary: The Valsalva maneuver decreases venous return and thus decreases blood flow through the aortic valve, making the AS murmur quieter.
explanation.whyCorrect: The intensity of the AS murmur depends on the amount of blood flowing through the stenotic aortic valve. The Valsalva maneuver (bearing down) decreases venous return to the heart, reducing blood flow through the aortic valve and making the murmur quieter. Similarly, standing decreases the murmur, while squatting increases it.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The Valsalva maneuver reduces venous return and decreases blood flow through the aortic valve, causing the murmur to become quieter, not louder. Squatting would increase the murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The AS murmur remains systolic regardless of maneuver because it is generated by turbulent flow during ventricular ejection through the stenotic valve; maneuvers only change its intensity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The Valsalva maneuver affects the murmur's intensity by changing blood flow volume but does not alter the anatomic radiation pattern of the aortic stenosis murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The AS murmur maintains its crescendo-decrescendo systolic quality during Valsalva; a continuous machinery-like murmur is characteristic of a patent ductus arteriosus, not aortic stenosis.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man with known severe aortic stenosis develops new-onset atrial fibrillation and bilateral pulmonary crackles on examination. An S4 gallop is audible, and his point of maximal impulse is displaced laterally. Echocardiography shows concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with preserved ejection fraction. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of his pulmonary symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Systolic heart failure with markedly reduced left ventricular contractility

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diastolic dysfunction from decreased ventricular compliance due to hypertrophy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acute mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle rupture

choices.label: D
choices.text: Primary pulmonary hypertension with right ventricular failure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acute respiratory distress syndrome from systemic inflammatory response

explanation.summary: Concentric LV hypertrophy from chronic AS causes decreased ventricular compliance and diastolic dysfunction (nonsystolic HF), leading to increased left atrial and pulmonary capillary pressures, causing pulmonary edema with crackles.
explanation.whyCorrect: In AS, chronic pressure overload causes concentric LV hypertrophy. The thickened ventricle has decreased compliance, leading to diastolic dysfunction (nonsystolic HF). This causes increased left atrial pressure and pulmonary capillary pressures, resulting in pulmonary edema (crackles) and atrial fibrillation. The preserved ejection fraction confirms diastolic rather than systolic HF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The preserved ejection fraction on echocardiography rules out systolic heart failure with reduced contractility. The patient's symptoms are from diastolic dysfunction due to the hypertrophied, noncompliant ventricle.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Acute mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle rupture would present acutely with a new murmur and hemodynamic instability, not with the chronic progressive findings of concentric hypertrophy and preserved EF.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Primary pulmonary hypertension is a disease of the pulmonary vasculature unrelated to aortic stenosis. The patient's pulmonary symptoms are explained by backpressure from the left heart due to diastolic dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: ARDS is caused by diffuse alveolar damage from systemic inflammation and would not present with the cardiac findings of concentric LV hypertrophy, S4 gallop, and laterally displaced PMI.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man from rural India presents with exertional dyspnea and a systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border. Echocardiography reveals thickened, calcified aortic and mitral valve leaflets with commissural fusion. He has a history of recurrent pharyngitis as a child that was never treated with antibiotics. Which of the following is the most likely etiology of his valvular disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Age-related degenerative calcification of a tricuspid aortic valve

choices.label: B
choices.text: Congenital bicuspid aortic valve with premature leaflet degeneration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Autoimmune valve damage from untreated Streptococcus pyogenes infection

choices.label: D
choices.text: Infective endocarditis with bacterial vegetations on the aortic valve

choices.label: E
choices.text: Carcinoid heart disease with fibrotic valve thickening

explanation.summary: Rheumatic heart disease from untreated Streptococcus pyogenes infection causes autoimmune damage to heart valves, most commonly the mitral and aortic valves, leading to scarring, calcification, and stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient's history of recurrent untreated pharyngitis in a developing country, combined with involvement of both the aortic and mitral valves with commissural fusion, is characteristic of rheumatic heart disease. Untreated Streptococcus pyogenes infection leads to rheumatic fever, where antibodies against the bacterial M protein cross-react with cardiac valve tissue, causing inflammation, scarring, and calcification.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Degenerative calcification of a normal tricuspid valve typically occurs in patients aged 70-80, not in a 40-year-old, and would not explain the concurrent mitral valve disease with commissural fusion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While a bicuspid valve can cause AS in the 40s-50s, it would not explain the concurrent mitral valve disease and commissural fusion pattern characteristic of rheumatic heart disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Infective endocarditis causes acute valve destruction with vegetations, not the chronic bilateral valve thickening with commissural fusion seen in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Carcinoid heart disease predominantly affects right-sided valves (tricuspid and pulmonary) due to hepatic metabolism of serotonin, not the left-sided valves involved in this case.

questions.stem: A 73-year-old man with severe aortic stenosis presents with angina during exercise. His cardiologist explains that coronary blood flow is reduced in AS, contributing to his symptoms. Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which aortic stenosis causes decreased coronary perfusion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The stenotic valve directs blood flow away from the coronary ostia during systole

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased flow from the LV through the stenotic valve reduces coronary artery filling pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic aortic stenosis causes intimal hyperplasia within the coronary arteries

choices.label: D
choices.text: Elevated right atrial pressure compresses the coronary sinus and impairs venous drainage

choices.label: E
choices.text: Turbulent flow across the stenotic valve causes microemboli that occlude coronary arteries

explanation.summary: In AS, the coronary arteries branch from the aorta immediately distal to the aortic valve. Decreased flow from the LV through the stenotic valve leads to decreased coronary blood flow and angina.
explanation.whyCorrect: The coronary arteries branch from the aorta immediately distal to the aortic valve at the very start of the ascending aorta. In aortic stenosis, decreased flow from the LV through the stenotic valve results in decreased coronary blood flow and coronary perfusion pressure, leading to myocardial ischemia and angina.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The coronary arteries fill primarily during diastole, not systole, and the mechanism of reduced coronary flow in AS is decreased antegrade flow through the stenotic valve reducing aortic root pressure, not redirection of flow.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aortic stenosis does not directly cause intimal hyperplasia in the coronary arteries. While AS and CAD share risk factors, the stenotic valve itself reduces coronary perfusion through decreased aortic flow, not intimal changes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Right atrial pressure and coronary sinus drainage are not directly affected by the aortic valve stenosis; the coronary perfusion deficit is caused by reduced flow through the stenotic valve into the aortic root.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Turbulent flow across the stenotic aortic valve does not cause microemboli. The reduced coronary perfusion results from decreased blood flow from the LV through the stenotic valve, lowering aortic root pressure.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset severe dyspnea and orthopnea that began 3 hours ago. He has a history of poorly controlled hypertension. His blood pressure is 180/50 mmHg, heart rate is 110 bpm, and respiratory rate is 28 breaths/min. Chest auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. Cardiac examination reveals a soft, high-pitched diastolic decrescendo murmur at the left sternal border. A CT angiogram of the chest reveals a dissection flap extending from the descending aorta into the ascending aorta. Which of the following is the most appropriate definitive management for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Initiation of hydralazine for afterload reduction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Serial echocardiographic monitoring every 6 months

choices.label: C
choices.text: Long-term therapy with an ACE inhibitor and diuretic

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aortic valve replacement surgery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Percutaneous balloon valvuloplasty of the aortic valve

explanation.summary: Acute aortic regurgitation from aortic dissection requires emergent aortic valve replacement as the definitive treatment.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acute AR from aortic dissection is a surgical emergency. The dissection has tracked to the ascending aorta, compromising the aortic valve and preventing leaflet coaptation. The only definitive treatment for symptomatic AR is aortic valve replacement (AVR), which is typically offered for acute AR such as from aortic dissection.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hydralazine is a direct vasodilator that can be used in chronic AR but is contraindicated in acute AR. Its rapid action does not allow adaptation, causing vasodilation that drops blood pressure and triggers increased heart rate, shortening diastole and potentially worsening regurgitation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Serial echocardiographic monitoring is appropriate for asymptomatic patients with mild chronic AR, not for acute, symptomatic AR from aortic dissection which requires emergent intervention.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ACE inhibitors are used in chronic symptomatic AR to reduce afterload and the regurgitant fraction, but they are not the definitive treatment and are insufficient for acute AR from dissection.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Percutaneous balloon valvuloplasty is not a standard treatment for aortic regurgitation. It may be used in aortic stenosis as a temporizing measure but does not address the regurgitant valve or the underlying dissection.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A 34-year-old man with a history of IV heroin use presents with fever, malaise, and a new diastolic murmur. Blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus. Echocardiography reveals vegetations on the aortic valve with moderate aortic regurgitation. His blood pressure is 145/55 mmHg. Which of the following best explains the widened pulse pressure observed in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased cardiac output from left ventricular systolic dysfunction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased systemic vascular resistance from septic vasoconstriction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vegetations on the valve increase forward flow velocity and raise peak systolic pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Compensatory concentric hypertrophy increases contractility and elevates systolic pressure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Regurgitant flow lowers diastolic pressure while increased end-diastolic volume raises stroke volume and systolic pressure

explanation.summary: In AR, blood regurgitates back into the LV during diastole lowering diastolic pressure, while the increased EDV increases stroke volume and systolic pressure via the Frank-Starling mechanism.
explanation.whyCorrect: The regurgitant fraction allows blood to flow back from the aorta into the LV during diastole, causing diastolic pressure to fall below normal. This regurgitant volume increases end-diastolic volume, which on the ascending limb of the Frank-Starling curve increases stroke volume and raises systolic pressure, producing a widened pulse pressure.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Decreased cardiac output would not cause a widened pulse pressure. In AR, cardiac output is initially maintained or increased due to the Frank-Starling mechanism compensating for the volume overload.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Septic vasoconstriction is not the mechanism here. The widened pulse pressure is due to the valvular regurgitation allowing diastolic runoff back into the ventricle, not changes in systemic vascular resistance.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Vegetations cause regurgitation, not increased forward flow velocity. Increased forward flow velocity causing elevated systolic pressure is characteristic of aortic stenosis, not regurgitation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: AR causes volume overload leading to eccentric hypertrophy (sarcomeres added in series), not concentric hypertrophy. Concentric hypertrophy is the compensatory response to pressure overload, as seen in aortic stenosis.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A 22-year-old tall, slender man with long fingers and hyperextensible joints is found to have a diastolic decrescendo murmur on a routine sports physical. Echocardiography reveals mild aortic regurgitation with a dilated aortic root. He is asymptomatic with a normal ejection fraction. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate referral for aortic valve replacement surgery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Observation with regular clinic visits and repeat echocardiograms

choices.label: C
choices.text: Initiation of high-dose nifedipine to reduce regurgitant fraction

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cardiac catheterization to quantify regurgitant volume

choices.label: E
choices.text: Initiation of a beta-blocker to slow the heart rate

explanation.summary: Asymptomatic patients with AR and preserved ejection fraction are managed with watchful waiting and serial echocardiography.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient likely has Marfan syndrome given his tall stature, long limbs, hyperextensible joints, and dilated aortic root. However, he has mild, asymptomatic AR with normal ejection fraction. The appropriate management for asymptomatic AR is observation with regular clinic visits and repeat echocardiograms, with timing ranging from every 3 to 5 years for mild disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aortic valve replacement is reserved for acute AR or chronic symptomatic AR. This patient is asymptomatic with mild disease and does not meet surgical indications.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Nifedipine is an afterload-reducing agent used for more advanced symptomatic AR. In asymptomatic, mild AR, medical therapy is not indicated as the initial management strategy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cardiac catheterization is not the standard next step. Echocardiography, including Doppler, is the primary tool for diagnosing and quantifying AR and has already been performed.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-blockers are not typically used in AR management. They could prolong diastole and theoretically worsen regurgitation by allowing more time for blood to flow back into the ventricle.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a 15-year history of chronic aortic regurgitation presents with progressive exertional dyspnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea over the past 6 months. Echocardiography shows a dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 35% and severe AR. Which of the following best describes the pathologic cardiac remodeling that has occurred in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Eccentric hypertrophy progressing to dilated cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Restrictive cardiomyopathy with impaired diastolic relaxation and normal wall thickness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Concentric hypertrophy with markedly increased wall thickness and normal chamber size

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy with asymmetric septal hypertrophy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Right ventricular hypertrophy from chronic pulmonary hypertension

explanation.summary: Chronic AR causes volume overload leading to eccentric hypertrophy, which eventually progresses to dilated cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) when compensation fails.
explanation.whyCorrect: In chronic AR, the volume overload causes the ventricle to dilate to accommodate the regurgitant volume. Initially, eccentric hypertrophy with some wall thickening compensates via the Law of Laplace. Eventually, wall thickening lags behind the increasing dilation, high wall tension destroys myocytes, and the result is dilated cardiomyopathy with decompensation causing heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Restrictive cardiomyopathy involves impaired diastolic relaxation from infiltrative diseases or fibrosis and is not the expected remodeling pattern from chronic AR.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Concentric hypertrophy with increased wall thickness and normal chamber size is the response to pressure overload, as seen in aortic stenosis or chronic hypertension, not the volume overload of AR.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy is a genetic condition with asymmetric septal hypertrophy and dynamic outflow tract obstruction, unrelated to the volume overload of AR.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Right ventricular hypertrophy from pulmonary hypertension is not the primary remodeling pattern in AR. AR directly affects the left ventricle through volume overload.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known moderate chronic aortic regurgitation is undergoing a physical examination. During dynamic auscultation, the physician asks the patient to perform a sustained handgrip. Which of the following changes in the patient's murmur would be expected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The murmur decreases in intensity due to decreased venous return

choices.label: B
choices.text: The murmur decreases in intensity due to increased forward flow

choices.label: C
choices.text: The murmur increases in intensity due to increased afterload

choices.label: D
choices.text: The murmur remains unchanged as handgrip does not affect diastolic murmurs

choices.label: E
choices.text: The murmur changes from diastolic to systolic due to altered hemodynamics

explanation.summary: Forceful handgrip increases afterload, which increases the intensity of left-sided regurgitant murmurs including AR.
explanation.whyCorrect: Forceful handgrip increases afterload by increasing systemic vascular resistance. This increases the pressure gradient driving blood back through the incompetent aortic valve during diastole, thus increasing left-sided regurgitant murmurs (AR, MR, and VSD murmurs).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Handgrip does not decrease venous return. Maneuvers that decrease venous return include standing and the Valsalva maneuver, which decrease most murmurs but do not specifically increase regurgitant murmurs.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While handgrip increases afterload, this does not cause increased forward flow through the aortic valve. Rather, the increased afterload increases the regurgitant fraction, making the murmur louder.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The AR murmur is not static during dynamic auscultation. Handgrip increases afterload, which increases the pressure gradient across the incompetent valve and intensifies the regurgitant murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Handgrip does not change the timing of the AR murmur. It remains a diastolic murmur because regurgitation occurs during diastole when the aortic valve should be closed.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents with exertional angina and dyspnea. On examination, his blood pressure is 165/45 mmHg. Carotid pulsations are bounding and rapidly collapse. An audible diastolic murmur is heard over the femoral artery when compressed with the stethoscope bell. Which of the following physical examination findings is also expected in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pulsus paradoxus with greater than 10 mmHg drop in systolic pressure during inspiration

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fixed splitting of the second heart sound throughout the respiratory cycle

choices.label: C
choices.text: A harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur radiating to the carotid arteries

choices.label: D
choices.text: A loud systolic murmur that increases with the Valsalva maneuver

choices.label: E
choices.text: A low-pitched, rumbling diastolic murmur best heard at the cardiac apex

explanation.summary: This patient has classic signs of AR with wide pulse pressure, water-hammer pulse, and Duroziez sign. An Austin-Flint murmur, a low rumbling diastolic murmur at the apex, is also associated with AR.
explanation.whyCorrect: The patient has aortic regurgitation evidenced by wide pulse pressure (165/45), water-hammer (Corrigan) pulse with bounding, rapidly collapsing carotid pulsations, and Duroziez sign (diastolic murmur over the femoral artery). An Austin-Flint murmur is a low, rumbling diastolic murmur best heard at the apex, caused by the regurgitant jet vibrating against the mitral valve.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulsus paradoxus is associated with cardiac tamponade and severe asthma, not aortic regurgitation. AR produces a widened pulse pressure, not pulsus paradoxus.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fixed splitting of S2 is characteristic of an atrial septal defect, not aortic regurgitation. AR does not cause fixed splitting of heart sounds.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A harsh crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur radiating to the carotids is characteristic of aortic stenosis, not aortic regurgitation. The murmur of AR is a diastolic decrescendo murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A systolic murmur that increases with Valsalva is characteristic of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, not AR. The Valsalva maneuver decreases venous return, which decreases most murmurs including AR.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of tertiary syphilis presents with exertional dyspnea. Examination reveals a diastolic decrescendo murmur at the left sternal border and a blood pressure of 170/55 mmHg. Echocardiography confirms severe aortic regurgitation. Which of the following is the underlying mechanism of AR in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoimmune destruction of aortic valve leaflets due to molecular mimicry

choices.label: B
choices.text: Congenital fusion of two aortic valve cusps leading to incomplete closure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Inflammatory aortitis causing dilation of the aortic root and loss of leaflet coaptation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Calcific degeneration of the aortic valve leaflets with progressive stiffening

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acute vegetative deposits on aortic valve leaflets from bacterial infection

explanation.summary: Syphilitic aortitis causes inflammation and dilation of the aortic root, which prevents the aortic valve cusps from coapting and results in chronic AR.
explanation.whyCorrect: Tertiary syphilis causes syphilitic aortitis, which leads to inflammation and dilation of the aortic root. Dilation of the aortic root is listed as a cause of chronic AR because stretching the part of the aorta that attaches to the heart and gives the valve its structure prevents the valve cusps from coapting properly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Autoimmune destruction due to molecular mimicry describes rheumatic heart disease from group A streptococcal infection, not syphilis. Syphilis causes aortitis with root dilation rather than direct valve leaflet destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.B: A congenital bicuspid aortic valve is a separate etiology of AR classified under aortic valve disease. It is not related to syphilis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Calcific degeneration of the valve leaflets occurs with aging and is an independent cause of AR. It is not the mechanism of AR in syphilitic aortitis, which affects the aortic root.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Acute vegetative deposits are characteristic of infective endocarditis, which causes acute AR. Syphilitic aortitis is a cause of chronic AR through root dilation, not vegetative deposits.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A cardiologist is evaluating a 60-year-old woman with moderate chronic aortic regurgitation who has developed worsening exertional dyspnea. Her blood pressure is 155/50 mmHg and ejection fraction is 50%. She is started on an ACE inhibitor. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which this medication improves her condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It increases heart rate, shortening diastole and reducing regurgitant time

choices.label: B
choices.text: It increases myocardial contractility, improving forward stroke volume

choices.label: C
choices.text: It reduces preload by causing venodilation and decreasing end-diastolic volume

choices.label: D
choices.text: It dilates arterial vessels, reducing afterload and the regurgitant fraction

choices.label: E
choices.text: It directly strengthens the aortic valve leaflets and improves coaptation

explanation.summary: ACE inhibitors reduce afterload by dilating arterial vessels, which decreases the pressure gradient driving regurgitant flow and reduces the regurgitant fraction in AR.
explanation.whyCorrect: ACE inhibitors dilate the arterial vessels, reducing the cardiac afterload and the regurgitant fraction. By decreasing systemic vascular resistance, less blood is driven back through the incompetent aortic valve during diastole, improving forward cardiac output.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ACE inhibitors do not significantly increase heart rate. Furthermore, shortening diastole to reduce regurgitant time is not their primary mechanism in AR management.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ACE inhibitors are not positive inotropes. They do not directly increase myocardial contractility. Their benefit comes from reducing afterload.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While ACE inhibitors may have some effect on preload, their primary therapeutic mechanism in AR is arterial vasodilation and afterload reduction, not venodilation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: No medication can directly strengthen aortic valve leaflets or improve their coaptation. The only definitive treatment to correct the valve itself is aortic valve replacement surgery.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A 12-year-old girl is referred to a pediatric cardiologist for evaluation of a systolic murmur detected during a school health screening. The murmur is best heard at the left upper sternal border at the second intercostal space. Echocardiography reveals a bicuspid pulmonary valve with fused commissures and a peak gradient of 55 mmHg across the valve. Which of the following best describes the expected clinical course if this condition remains untreated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Progressive left atrial enlargement leading to atrial fibrillation and stroke

choices.label: B
choices.text: Progressive right heart failure with jugular venous distention, peripheral edema, and ascites

choices.label: C
choices.text: Eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy progressing to dilated cardiomyopathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Development of pulmonary regurgitation with secondary mitral stenosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aortic root dilation with subsequent aortic valve incompetence

explanation.summary: Severe congenital pulmonary stenosis, if untreated, can lead to right-sided heart failure with classic signs including jugular venous distention, peripheral edema, liver swelling, and ascites.
explanation.whyCorrect: The most common pulmonary valve disease is congenital pulmonary stenosis. In cases of severe valve disorder (often with congenital PS), right-sided heart failure can result. Classic signs and symptoms of right heart failure include jugular venous distention, peripheral edema, liver swelling, abdominal ascites, and fatigue.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Left atrial enlargement, atrial fibrillation, and stroke are complications of left-sided valvular disease such as mitral stenosis, not right-sided pulmonary stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Eccentric left ventricular hypertrophy and dilated cardiomyopathy result from left-sided volume overload conditions like aortic regurgitation, not from pulmonary valve stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pulmonary regurgitation from pulmonary hypertension-related dilation of the pulmonary artery is a separate entity. Secondary mitral stenosis is not a consequence of pulmonary valve disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic root dilation and aortic valve incompetence are features of conditions affecting the aorta and aortic valve (e.g., Marfan syndrome, hypertension), not consequences of pulmonary stenosis.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with chronic severe aortic regurgitation presents to a clinic for routine follow-up. She has been asymptomatic but her latest echocardiogram shows a progressively dilating left ventricle. The physician explains the compensatory mechanism that has maintained her cardiac function. According to the Law of Laplace, which of the following best describes the initial compensatory response of her left ventricle to the volume overload?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Concentric hypertrophy with sarcomeres added in parallel to increase wall thickness proportionally

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chamber dilation without any hypertrophy, relying solely on the Frank-Starling mechanism

choices.label: C
choices.text: Myocardial fibrosis replacing damaged myocytes to maintain structural integrity of the chamber

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased heart rate as the primary compensatory mechanism to maintain cardiac output

choices.label: E
choices.text: Eccentric hypertrophy with some wall thickening to partially compensate for increased wall stress from dilation

explanation.summary: In volume overload from AR, the initial compensatory response is eccentric hypertrophy with some wall thickening, which helps offset the increased wall stress from ventricular dilation per the Law of Laplace.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per the Law of Laplace (Wall Tension = Pressure x Radius / 2 x Wall Thickness), ventricular dilation increases radius and wall stress. In the initial stage of volume overload, eccentric hypertrophy includes some wall thickening (not as much as concentric hypertrophy), which compensates for the increasing wall stress caused by increased radius and helps maintain pumping ability for the larger volume.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Concentric hypertrophy with sarcomeres added in parallel is the response to pressure overload, as seen in aortic stenosis. AR causes volume overload, which leads to eccentric hypertrophy with sarcomeres added in series.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While chamber dilation occurs, it is not without any hypertrophy. The initial compensatory phase includes eccentric hypertrophy with wall thickening to offset the increased wall stress from the larger radius.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myocardial fibrosis is a pathologic process, not a compensatory mechanism. It occurs in advanced disease states and contributes to dysfunction rather than maintaining structural integrity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Increased heart rate is not the primary compensatory mechanism in chronic AR. The Frank-Starling mechanism and eccentric hypertrophy are the main adaptations that maintain cardiac output.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with a history of carcinoid syndrome presents with progressive fatigue and lower extremity edema. On cardiac examination, a diastolic murmur is heard at the left upper sternal border. Echocardiography reveals mild pulmonary regurgitation and moderate tricuspid regurgitation. Which of the following best explains why the tricuspid valve is more severely affected than the pulmonary valve in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The right ventricle dilates more readily than the pulmonary artery, making secondary tricuspid regurgitation more common

choices.label: B
choices.text: The pulmonary valve is structurally stronger due to its semilunar configuration

choices.label: C
choices.text: Carcinoid syndrome preferentially affects left-sided valves before right-sided valves

choices.label: D
choices.text: The tricuspid valve has higher exposure to serotonin metabolites as the first valve in the venous series

choices.label: E
choices.text: The pulmonary valve is protected by the high pressures in the pulmonary circulation

explanation.summary: In carcinoid syndrome, while both right-sided valves are exposed, the tricuspid valve is more commonly affected because the RV dilates more readily than the pulmonary artery, making secondary TR more common than PR.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carcinoid syndrome can affect the right-sided valves, but affects the tricuspid valve more than the pulmonic valve. The source material explains that the RV dilates more readily than the pulmonary artery, making secondary TR more common than PR.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Both the pulmonary and aortic valves are semilunar valves. The source does not attribute less pulmonary valve involvement to structural superiority of the semilunar configuration.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Carcinoid syndrome affects right-sided valves, not left-sided valves. The serotonin metabolites are inactivated by the lungs before reaching the left heart.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While the tricuspid valve is the first valve in the right-sided series, the source material attributes the difference to the RV dilating more readily than the pulmonary artery, making secondary TR more common, rather than differential serotonin exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The pulmonary circulation is a low-pressure system, not a high-pressure system. The low pressure on the right side actually makes significant isolated pulmonary valve disease less common overall.

questions.topicName: Aortic Regurgitation and Pulmonary Valve Disease
questions.stem: A physician is teaching medical students about auscultation of the aortic regurgitation murmur. She instructs a student to position the patient optimally to hear the murmur. Which of the following combinations of patient position and respiratory phase best augments the AR murmur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Supine position during deep inspiration with the stethoscope at the apex

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left lateral decubitus position during expiration with the stethoscope at the apex

choices.label: C
choices.text: Standing upright during inspiration with the stethoscope at the right lower sternal border

choices.label: D
choices.text: Sitting up and leaning forward at end-expiration with the stethoscope at the left third intercostal space

choices.label: E
choices.text: Supine with legs elevated during inspiration with the stethoscope at the left axilla

explanation.summary: The AR murmur is best heard with the patient sitting up and leaning forward at end-expiration, auscultated at Erb's point (left 3rd intercostal space parasternal border).
explanation.whyCorrect: The AR murmur is best auscultated at the parasternal border at either the right 2nd intercostal space or left 3rd intercostal space (Erb's point) at the end of expiration with the patient sitting up and leaning forward. Expiration decreases lung volume and increases venous return to the left heart, augmenting the left-sided murmur (RILE: Right-Inspiration, Left-Expiration).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Inspiration increases right-sided murmurs but decreases left-sided murmurs due to blood pooling in the expanded lungs. The apex is also not the optimal location for the AR murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Left lateral decubitus at the apex is the optimal position for hearing the mitral stenosis murmur, not the AR murmur. While expiration is correct for left-sided murmurs, the location and position are wrong.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Standing decreases venous return and diminishes most murmurs. Inspiration decreases left-sided murmurs. The right lower sternal border is not the optimal auscultation point for AR.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While leg elevation increases venous return, inspiration decreases left-sided murmurs. The left axilla is the location for hearing mitral regurgitation, not AR.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman who recently immigrated from South Asia presents at 30 weeks gestation with acute-onset dyspnea and pink frothy sputum. Her heart rate is 120 bpm and blood pressure is 100/70 mmHg. Cardiac auscultation reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm with a low-pitched diastolic rumble at the apex. She has no prior cardiac history. Which of the following best explains why this patient developed pulmonary edema during pregnancy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pregnancy-induced hypertension caused acute left ventricular failure and flash pulmonary edema

choices.label: B
choices.text: The increased blood volume and heart rate of pregnancy overwhelmed the stenotic mitral valve, increasing left atrial pressure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pregnancy caused de novo mitral valve calcification from hormonal changes and calcium mobilization

choices.label: D
choices.text: The physiologic anemia of pregnancy reduced oxygen delivery, causing myocardial ischemia and pump failure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Gestational diabetes resulted in osmotic fluid shifts that accumulated in the pulmonary vasculature

explanation.summary: In pregnancy, a 30-50% increase in blood volume and 20% increase in heart rate worsen MS by forcing more blood through the stenotic valve and shortening diastolic filling time, leading to increased LA pressure and pulmonary edema.
explanation.whyCorrect: Normal hemodynamic changes of pregnancy include a 30-50% increase in blood volume and a 20% increase in heart rate. The increased blood volume is deleterious in MS because a larger volume of blood is trying to get through the stenotic mitral valve. The increased heart rate shortens diastole, further impairing ventricular filling. Previously asymptomatic patients often present with symptoms during pregnancy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: There is no evidence of hypertension in this patient (BP 100/70). The pulmonary edema is due to the stenotic mitral valve being unable to accommodate the physiologic volume and heart rate changes of pregnancy, not primary LV failure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pregnancy does not cause de novo mitral valve calcification. This patient most likely has rheumatic heart disease from a childhood streptococcal infection, the most common cause of MS, especially in immigrants from developing countries.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While mild physiologic anemia occurs in pregnancy, it does not typically cause myocardial ischemia. The pulmonary edema is from elevated left atrial pressures behind a stenotic mitral valve.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Gestational diabetes does not cause pulmonary edema through osmotic fluid shifts. The mechanism here is mechanical obstruction at the stenotic mitral valve with elevated left atrial and pulmonary venous pressures.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known mitral stenosis from rheumatic heart disease presents with a 2-week history of progressive exertional dyspnea. On examination, her heart rate is 130 bpm with an irregularly irregular rhythm. ECG confirms atrial fibrillation. Previously, her murmur had a presystolic accentuation, but this finding is now absent. Which of the following best explains the disappearance of presystolic accentuation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mitral valve has become so severely stenotic that no blood can pass through it

choices.label: B
choices.text: The tachycardia has caused the murmur to shift from diastole to systole

choices.label: C
choices.text: Loss of organized atrial contraction eliminates the late diastolic augmentation of flow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Atrial fibrillation has caused the mitral valve leaflets to calcify more rapidly

choices.label: E
choices.text: The increased heart rate has improved forward flow, eliminating the pressure gradient

explanation.summary: Presystolic accentuation is caused by increased flow across the stenotic valve during atrial contraction (atrial kick). In atrial fibrillation, organized atrial contraction is lost, so presystolic accentuation disappears.
explanation.whyCorrect: The diastolic murmur of MS has a decrescendo-crescendo pattern. It slows during passive ventricular filling then increases with atrial contraction (presystolic accentuation). When atrial fibrillation develops, the atrial kick is lost, and presystolic accentuation disappears.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Complete obstruction of the mitral valve would be incompatible with life. The patient still has MS with a murmur; it is the presystolic accentuation specifically that has disappeared.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Tachycardia does not cause the murmur to shift timing. The MS murmur remains diastolic. The change is specifically the loss of the late diastolic crescendo component that was caused by atrial contraction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Atrial fibrillation does not accelerate valvular calcification. The disappearance of presystolic accentuation is an immediate hemodynamic consequence of losing organized atrial contraction, not a structural change.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Increased heart rate actually worsens forward flow in MS because diastole shortens, reducing filling time. The pressure gradient across the stenotic valve increases, not decreases, with tachycardia.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with a history of rheumatic fever in childhood presents with sudden-onset right-sided weakness and expressive aphasia. CT of the head reveals an acute ischemic stroke in the left middle cerebral artery territory. Echocardiography shows a stenotic mitral valve with a dilated left atrium and a thrombus in the left atrial appendage. ECG reveals atrial fibrillation. Which of the following pathophysiologic mechanisms most directly led to this patient's stroke?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque in the left internal carotid artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale from deep vein thrombosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Calcific debris from the stenotic mitral valve embolizing to the cerebral vasculature

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood stasis in the dilated left atrium with atrial fibrillation leading to thrombus formation and embolization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vasospasm of the middle cerebral artery triggered by elevated left atrial pressure

explanation.summary: MS causes left atrial dilation and atrial fibrillation, which leads to blood stasis, thrombus formation in the LA, and cardioembolic stroke when the thrombus embolizes to the cerebral vasculature.
explanation.whyCorrect: In atrial dilation and atrial fibrillation, the lack of regular atrial contractions allows blood to remain static in the LA, increasing the risk of atrial thrombi formation. These thrombi can travel downstream from the LA and exit the heart to occlude the arterial blood supply, most often resulting in a cardioembolic stroke.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While atherosclerotic carotid disease is a common cause of stroke, this patient has clear evidence of a cardioembolic source with LA thrombus, dilated LA, MS, and atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Paradoxical embolism through a PFO involves venous thrombi crossing to the arterial circulation. This patient has a clear left atrial source of thrombus from MS with atrial fibrillation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While calcific emboli can theoretically occur, the primary mechanism of stroke in MS is thromboembolism from blood stasis in the fibrillating, dilated left atrium, as evidenced by the LA thrombus seen on echocardiography.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vasospasm of cerebral arteries is not a recognized mechanism of stroke in mitral stenosis. The stroke is caused by mechanical occlusion from an embolized thrombus.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with known severe mitral stenosis and atrial fibrillation is being evaluated for definitive treatment. Transesophageal echocardiography shows favorable valve morphology with pliable leaflets, minimal calcification, no significant subvalvular disease, and no left atrial thrombus. Which of the following is the most appropriate interventional procedure for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Percutaneous mitral balloon commissurotomy via transseptal catheterization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Open mitral valve replacement with a mechanical prosthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Transcatheter aortic valve replacement with subsequent mitral repair

choices.label: D
choices.text: Percutaneous mitral valve clip placement to reduce regurgitation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Open surgical mitral annuloplasty with ring placement

explanation.summary: Percutaneous mitral balloon commissurotomy is the preferred interventional procedure for severe MS when valve morphology is favorable, performed via femoral vein access with transseptal puncture.
explanation.whyCorrect: During percutaneous mitral balloon commissurotomy (valvotomy), a balloon-tipped catheter is inserted via the femoral vein into the right atrium, a transseptal puncture advances the catheter to the left atrium and across the mitral valve, and the valve is dilated by balloon inflation. This is performed in those with severe MS who have favorable valvular anatomy as determined by echocardiographic scoring.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Open mitral valve replacement is now less common and reserved for cases with extensive valve damage or unfavorable anatomy for percutaneous intervention. This patient has favorable morphology.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Transcatheter aortic valve replacement addresses aortic valve disease and is irrelevant to mitral stenosis. This patient's pathology is isolated to the mitral valve.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Percutaneous mitral valve clip placement (e.g., MitraClip) is used for mitral regurgitation, not mitral stenosis. Clipping would worsen stenosis by further reducing the valve orifice area.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mitral annuloplasty with ring placement is a surgical technique used for mitral regurgitation to reduce annular dilation. It does not address the commissural fusion that causes rheumatic mitral stenosis.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with a history of rheumatic heart disease presents with worsening dyspnea, lower extremity edema, jugular venous distention, and hepatomegaly. Echocardiography reveals severe mitral stenosis with a valve area of 0.9 cm², elevated pulmonary artery pressures, and right ventricular dilation. Which of the following is the most likely sequence of events leading to this patient's right heart failure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mitral stenosis caused left ventricular dilation, which compressed the right ventricle and impaired its function

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rheumatic disease simultaneously damaged the tricuspid valve, causing primary right ventricular failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chronic atrial fibrillation from mitral stenosis directly caused right ventricular myocardial fibrosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mitral stenosis reduced cardiac output, causing renal hypoperfusion and fluid overload affecting the right heart

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mitral stenosis elevated left atrial pressure, increasing pulmonary capillary and artery pressures, leading to right ventricular pressure overload

explanation.summary: Severe MS elevates left atrial pressure, which transmits back to the pulmonary capillaries and pulmonary arteries, eventually causing right ventricular pressure overload and right heart failure.
explanation.whyCorrect: In severe MS, increased pulmonary capillary pressures from elevated LA pressure then increase pulmonary artery and RV pressures. This may, in severe cases, lead to findings of right heart failure like jugular venous distension, lower extremity edema, and hepatomegaly.

whyWrongByChoice.A: MS does not cause left ventricular dilation. In fact, the LV is typically normal or underfilled in MS because the stenotic valve restricts flow into the LV. The right heart failure is from pulmonary hypertension.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While rheumatic disease can affect multiple valves, the question describes isolated severe MS with secondary pulmonary hypertension as the mechanism. The right heart failure is from RV pressure overload, not primary tricuspid disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atrial fibrillation does not directly cause right ventricular myocardial fibrosis. The right heart failure results from elevated pulmonary pressures transmitted backward from the stenotic mitral valve.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While reduced cardiac output can occur in severe MS, the mechanism of right heart failure is primarily through elevated pulmonary pressures causing RV pressure overload, not through renal hypoperfusion and fluid retention.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with mitral stenosis and newly diagnosed atrial fibrillation is seen in the cardiology clinic. She reports increasing fatigue and exertional dyspnea over the past 3 months. Her heart rate is 105 bpm. The cardiologist prescribes metoprolol and warfarin. Which of the following best explains why beta-blocker therapy improves this patient's symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It decreases myocardial oxygen demand, preventing ischemia-induced left ventricular dysfunction

choices.label: B
choices.text: It increases the force of left atrial contraction, improving forward flow across the stenotic valve

choices.label: C
choices.text: It slows the heart rate, prolonging diastole and allowing more time for blood to flow through the stenotic valve

choices.label: D
choices.text: It reduces systemic vascular resistance, decreasing the afterload on the left ventricle

choices.label: E
choices.text: It directly relaxes the stenotic mitral valve leaflets, increasing the valve orifice area

explanation.summary: Beta-blockers slow the heart rate, which prolongs diastole. Since MS is an obstruction during diastole that inhibits passive filling, a slower heart rate allows more time for blood to flow through the stenotic valve.
explanation.whyCorrect: MS is an obstruction during diastole that inhibits passive filling of blood from the LA to the LV. As heart rate increases and the cardiac cycle shortens, nearly all of the time is subtracted from the diastolic phase. Slowing the heart rate with beta-blockers allows more time for blood to flow through during diastole, reducing symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While beta-blockers do decrease myocardial oxygen demand, this is not the primary mechanism of benefit in MS. The key therapeutic effect is prolonging diastolic filling time.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta-blockers do not increase the force of atrial contraction. Furthermore, this patient has atrial fibrillation and has lost effective atrial contraction entirely.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Beta-blockers do not primarily reduce systemic vascular resistance. Their benefit in MS is through heart rate control, not afterload reduction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: No medication directly relaxes or opens stenotic mitral valve leaflets. The stenosis is a structural problem requiring an invasive procedure for definitive treatment.

questions.stem: A pathologist examines a heart specimen from a 55-year-old man who died of complications from valvular heart disease. The mitral valve shows thickened, calcified leaflets with fibrin deposits on the cusps and fusion of the commissures, creating a slit-like, curved opening. Which of the following is the most likely underlying etiology of this valve pathology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Age-related degenerative calcification of the mitral annulus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infective endocarditis with Streptococcus viridans causing valve destruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Systemic lupus erythematosus with Libman-Sacks endocarditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chronic rheumatic heart disease from inadequately treated streptococcal infection

choices.label: E
choices.text: Myxomatous degeneration of the mitral valve with prolapse

explanation.summary: The fish-mouth appearance with thickened, calcified leaflets, fibrin deposits, and commissural fusion is the characteristic pathologic finding of rheumatic mitral stenosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic inflammation from rheumatic heart disease leads to thickened, calcified leaflets with fibrin deposits on the cusps causing stenosis. Severe inflammation results in commissural fusion, where the leaflets fuse together at the commissures, producing the characteristic slit-like, curved opening resembling a fish mouth.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Age-related calcification of the mitral annulus more commonly interferes with leaflet closure causing mitral regurgitation. In advanced cases it can cause stenosis, but does not produce commissural fusion or the characteristic fish-mouth appearance.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Infective endocarditis causes vegetations and valve destruction, typically leading to regurgitation rather than stenosis. It does not produce the commissural fusion and fish-mouth appearance characteristic of rheumatic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Libman-Sacks endocarditis in SLE causes small, sterile vegetations on both surfaces of the valve. It does not typically cause the commissural fusion and calcification pattern described here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Myxomatous degeneration causes redundant, floppy valve tissue leading to mitral valve prolapse and regurgitation, not the thickened, calcified, fused leaflets seen in rheumatic stenosis.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with known moderate mitral stenosis undergoes echocardiography that shows a mitral valve area of 1.2 cm². Her left ventricular ejection fraction is 60% and left ventricular end-diastolic volume is normal. She has mild exertional dyspnea but is otherwise functional. Analysis of her pressure-volume loop would most likely show which of the following compared to a normal heart?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased left ventricular end-diastolic volume with a rightward shift of the loop

choices.label: B
choices.text: Normal left ventricular volumes and pressures in most cases of moderate MS

choices.label: C
choices.text: Markedly increased left ventricular peak systolic pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreased left ventricular end-systolic volume with increased ejection fraction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Widened pressure-volume loop indicating increased stroke work

explanation.summary: In most cases of MS, the LV is not affected and LV volumes and pressures remain normal. Significant LV changes occur only in severe MS.
explanation.whyCorrect: In most cases of MS, the LV is not affected, and LV volumes and pressures remain normal. The primary pathology in MS is upstream from the LV, in the left atrium and pulmonary vasculature. Only in severe MS does decreased LV end-diastolic volume occur from restricted filling.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased LV end-diastolic volume would be seen in volume overload conditions like aortic or mitral regurgitation, not in mitral stenosis where filling is restricted.
