choices.text: To prevent post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis from developing as a secondary complication of ARF

choices.label: E
choices.text: To suppress the ongoing autoimmune response by reducing the overall bacterial burden in the oropharynx

explanation.summary: Long-term antibiotic prophylaxis with penicillin (or erythromycin if allergic) after ARF is continued for 5–10 years to prevent recurrent streptococcal pharyngitis, which places patients at high risk for another episode of ARF and markedly worsens the risk of developing chronic RHD.
explanation.whyCorrect: Recurrent GAS pharyngitis triggers additional autoimmune attacks on already-damaged heart valves. Each recurrence of ARF worsens valve scarring and significantly increases the risk of chronic RHD. Prophylactic antibiotics prevent reinfection with GAS, thereby preventing recurrent ARF episodes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This describes the rationale for infective endocarditis prophylaxis, not ARF secondary prevention. The purpose in ARF is to prevent new strep throat infections, not to prevent direct valve colonization.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The prophylaxis is aimed at preventing new streptococcal infections, not eradicating latent organisms. The initial treatment with penicillin eradicates the existing infection; the long-term prophylaxis prevents reinfection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is a separate complication of GAS infection and is not prevented by long-term antibiotic prophylaxis after ARF. The prophylaxis specifically targets prevention of recurrent ARF and RHD.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The purpose is not to suppress an ongoing autoimmune response but to prevent new GAS infections that would trigger new autoimmune episodes. Once the strep infection is cleared, the autoimmune reaction resolves on its own.

questions.topicName: Acute Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease
questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy from a rural community presents with fever of 39°C and migratory joint pain involving his ankles and wrists for 5 days. Three weeks ago, he had pharyngitis. On examination, he has a pansystolic murmur loudest at the apex, and small, firm, painless nodules are palpated over the extensor surface of both elbows. Throat culture is negative. Which of the following serologic tests would be most useful in confirming the etiology of his current illness?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Serum rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody levels

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antinuclear antibody titer and complement levels (C3 and C4)

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antistreptolysin O titer and anti-DNase B antibody levels

choices.label: D
choices.text: Blood cultures obtained from two separate venipuncture sites

choices.label: E
choices.text: Serum procalcitonin level and peripheral blood smear for toxic granulations

explanation.summary: This patient has clinical features consistent with ARF (migratory arthritis, carditis with apical pansystolic murmur, subcutaneous nodules) following pharyngitis. The Jones criteria require evidence of recent GAS infection. Since throat cultures are often negative by the time of ARF presentation, streptococcal serology (ASO titer, anti-DNase B) is the most useful test to document recent infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer and anti-DNase B antibody levels are streptococcal serologic tests that document recent GAS infection. Since throat cultures are generally normal by the time ARF presents (weeks after the initial pharyngitis), serology is the most reliable method to confirm streptococcal etiology.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are used to diagnose rheumatoid arthritis, not ARF. While both can cause joint symptoms, the clinical picture with post-pharyngitis onset, carditis, and subcutaneous nodules points to ARF.
whyWrongByChoice.B: ANA and complement levels are used to evaluate systemic lupus erythematosus and other autoimmune conditions. These would not help confirm a recent streptococcal infection needed for the Jones criteria.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Blood cultures are used to diagnose bacteremia and infective endocarditis, not ARF. In ARF, the heart is damaged by autoimmune cross-reactivity, not by direct bacterial infection of the valves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Procalcitonin and toxic granulations help assess bacterial infection severity but do not specifically identify streptococcal infection. They would not satisfy the Jones criteria requirement for evidence of recent GAS infection.

questions.topicName: Acute Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease
questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with a history of recurrent untreated streptococcal pharyngitis in childhood presents with exertional dyspnea and fatigue. On cardiac auscultation, a systolic crescendo-decrescendo ejection murmur is heard best at the upper right sternal border. His radial pulse is weak and delayed. Echocardiography confirms severe valvular disease. Which of the following pathologic processes most directly led to his current valve abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Myxomatous degeneration of the valve leaflets with secondary calcification

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chronic fibrosis and scarring of the valve from recurrent autoimmune inflammation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bacterial vegetation formation with subsequent valve perforation and regurgitation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Congenital fusion of valve commissures with progressive calcific degeneration

choices.label: E
choices.text: Atherosclerotic plaque formation within the valve cusps causing flow obstruction

explanation.summary: This patient has aortic stenosis (crescendo-decrescendo ejection murmur at the upper right sternal border with pulsus tardus et parvus) secondary to chronic RHD. The history of recurrent untreated streptococcal pharyngitis and his relatively young age for aortic stenosis point to RHD as the cause.
explanation.whyCorrect: In chronic RHD, recurrent episodes of ARF cause repeated autoimmune-mediated inflammation of the aortic valve. Over many years, this leads to progressive fibrosis and scarring, eventually resulting in aortic stenosis. The characteristic crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the upper right sternal border with a delayed, weak pulse (pulsus tardus et parvus) confirms aortic stenosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Myxomatous degeneration is associated with mitral valve prolapse, not aortic stenosis. It causes leaflet redundancy and regurgitation rather than stenosis from fibrotic scarring.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bacterial vegetations describe infective endocarditis, which causes valve destruction and regurgitation, not the chronic fibrotic stenosis seen in RHD. ARF involves autoimmune damage, not direct infection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Congenital fusion of commissures describes a bicuspid aortic valve. While this can lead to calcific aortic stenosis, the history of recurrent untreated streptococcal pharyngitis strongly suggests RHD as the etiology.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Atherosclerotic plaque formation does not occur within valve cusps. Degenerative calcific aortic stenosis in elderly patients involves calcification and lipid deposition, but this patient is too young for that etiology.

questions.topicName: Acute Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease
questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl is evaluated for a 2-week history of fever, fatigue, and joint swelling. She was treated for pharyngitis with amoxicillin 4 weeks ago. On examination, she has a blowing early diastolic murmur at the left sternal border and an erythematous, ring-shaped, nonpruritic rash on her trunk that is evanescent. Her ESR is 72 mm/hr, and CRP is 5.2 mg/dL. The early diastolic murmur heard on this patient's examination is most consistent with which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mitral stenosis from fibrotic thickening of the mitral valve leaflets and chordae tendineae

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mitral regurgitation from autoimmune-mediated destruction of the mitral valve apparatus

choices.label: C
choices.text: Aortic regurgitation from immune-mediated damage to the aortic valve during acute inflammation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Aortic stenosis from progressive calcification and narrowing of the aortic valve orifice

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tricuspid regurgitation from right ventricular dilation secondary to pulmonary hypertension

explanation.summary: In acute rheumatic fever, aortic regurgitation presents as a blowing, early diastolic murmur audible at the left sternal border. This occurs due to immune-mediated damage to the aortic valve during the acute inflammatory phase of ARF.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aortic regurgitation is a recognized cardiac manifestation of ARF. It classically presents as a blowing, early diastolic murmur audible at the left sternal border. The source material specifically identifies this murmur as characteristic of aortic regurgitation in ARF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mitral stenosis produces a diastolic murmur with an opening snap heard at the apex, not at the left sternal border. Additionally, mitral stenosis is a late complication of chronic RHD, not typically seen during acute ARF.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mitral regurgitation in ARF produces a pansystolic murmur loudest at the apex, not an early diastolic murmur at the left sternal border.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aortic stenosis produces a systolic crescendo-decrescendo ejection murmur at the upper right sternal border, not a diastolic murmur. Aortic stenosis is also a chronic complication of RHD, not an acute finding.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tricuspid regurgitation produces a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration, not a diastolic murmur at the left sternal border.

questions.topicName: Acute Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease
questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy from a low-income urban community develops acute rheumatic fever following untreated streptococcal pharyngitis. His physician explains to the family that the disease is caused by an autoimmune mechanism. Which of the following best describes the specific immunologic process responsible for the cardiac damage in this condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation causing histamine release and vascular permeability in the endocardium

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deposition of circulating antigen-antibody complexes in the endocardium activating the complement cascade

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antibodies generated against bacterial M protein cross-reacting with myosin in cardiomyocytes via molecular mimicry

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cytotoxic CD8+ T lymphocytes directly recognizing and destroying cardiomyocytes expressing streptococcal antigens

choices.label: E
choices.text: Superantigen-induced polyclonal T-cell activation causing widespread nonspecific tissue damage to the myocardium

explanation.summary: ARF is a type II hypersensitivity reaction caused by molecular mimicry. Antibodies generated against the M protein of Streptococcus pyogenes cross-react with myosin found in cardiomyocytes because M protein shares epitopes with human myosin.
explanation.whyCorrect: The cardiac damage in ARF occurs through a type II hypersensitivity mechanism. The M protein virulence factor of S. pyogenes resembles and shares antibody epitopes with human myosin in cardiomyocytes. Autoantibodies generated against M protein cross-react with endocardial proteins through molecular mimicry, initiating antibody-mediated cytotoxic reactions that cause inflammation and tissue injury.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation describes a type I hypersensitivity reaction (e.g., anaphylaxis, allergic rhinitis). ARF is a type II hypersensitivity reaction involving antibody cross-reactivity, not IgE-mediated processes.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Immune complex deposition describes a type III hypersensitivity reaction (e.g., serum sickness, post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis). ARF specifically involves type II hypersensitivity with direct antibody binding to host cell antigens.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While CD4+ T cells play a role in forming Aschoff bodies, the primary mechanism is antibody-mediated (type II hypersensitivity), not CD8+ T-cell-mediated cytotoxicity. The antibodies cross-react with host proteins, not with streptococcal antigens on cardiomyocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Superantigen-mediated polyclonal T-cell activation is associated with toxic shock syndrome, not ARF. The mechanism in ARF is specific antibody cross-reactivity through molecular mimicry between M protein and myosin.

questions.topicName: Acute Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease
questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with known rheumatic heart disease and symptomatic mitral stenosis is being evaluated for surgical options. She reports progressive exertional dyspnea, now occurring with minimal activity, and has developed paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. Her echocardiogram shows severe mitral stenosis with a valve area of 0.9 cm², left atrial dilation, and estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 65 mmHg. If this patient does not receive definitive surgical treatment, what is her expected prognosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Greater than 90% ten-year survival with medical management alone given her young age

choices.label: B
choices.text: Median survival of approximately 3 years due to the development of pulmonary hypertension

choices.label: C
choices.text: Progressive improvement over the next decade as the autoimmune process spontaneously resolves

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stable clinical course with a 50% ten-year survival rate with optimized medical therapy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Rapid deterioration within 6 months with near-certain mortality without emergent transplantation

explanation.summary: Patients with symptomatic chronic RHD who develop pulmonary hypertension have a particularly poor prognosis. The source material states that median survival falls to approximately 3 years once pulmonary hypertension develops, highlighting the need for valve replacement.
explanation.whyCorrect: According to the source material, if limiting symptoms develop in chronic RHD, 10-year survival drops to 15% or less without treatment. If pulmonary hypertension develops, the median survival falls to as low as 3 years. This patient has both severe symptoms and elevated pulmonary pressures (65 mmHg), indicating pulmonary hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Greater than 10-year survival with medical management applies to asymptomatic patients with RHD. This patient has severe symptoms and pulmonary hypertension, conferring a much worse prognosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RHD is a chronic, progressive condition caused by irreversible fibrosis and scarring of the valve. The autoimmune process from the initial ARF may have resolved, but the structural damage persists and worsens over time.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A 50% ten-year survival rate is overly optimistic for a patient with symptomatic RHD and pulmonary hypertension. The source material states 10-year survival is 15% or less once limiting symptoms develop.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While the prognosis is poor, immediate death within 6 months is overly pessimistic. The median survival with pulmonary hypertension is approximately 3 years, and valve replacement (not transplantation) is the appropriate surgical intervention.

questions.topicName: Acute Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease
questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy presents with acute onset fever, chest pain, and bilateral knee swelling 3 weeks after an episode of pharyngitis. Physical examination reveals a friction rub best heard with the patient leaning forward, as well as a holosystolic murmur at the apex. Echocardiography demonstrates a small pericardial effusion and moderate mitral regurgitation. Laboratory studies reveal elevated ASO titers. Which of the following best describes the extent of cardiac involvement in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Isolated valvulitis limited to the mitral valve without involvement of other cardiac structures

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pancarditis involving the pericardium, myocardium, epicardium, and valves simultaneously

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pericarditis with secondary mitral valve prolapse due to increased pericardial fluid pressure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Myocarditis with papillary muscle dysfunction causing functional mitral regurgitation only

choices.label: E
choices.text: Infective endocarditis with bacterial vegetations causing both valve destruction and pericardial spread

explanation.summary: ARF can involve most of the heart simultaneously, including the pericardium (friction rub, pericardial effusion), myocardium, epicardium, and valves (mitral regurgitation). This patient demonstrates pancarditis, a recognized pattern in ARF.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that ARF can involve most of the heart, including the pericardium, myocardium, epicardium, and valves. This patient has evidence of pericardial involvement (friction rub, pericardial effusion) and valvular involvement (mitral regurgitation), consistent with pancarditis in the setting of confirmed streptococcal infection and ARF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The friction rub and pericardial effusion indicate pericardial involvement beyond just the mitral valve. ARF characteristically can cause pancarditis affecting multiple cardiac layers.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The mitral regurgitation in ARF is caused by immune-mediated valve damage, not secondary to pericardial fluid compression. Pericardial effusion does not cause mitral valve prolapse.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While myocarditis may be present, the friction rub indicates pericarditis as well, and the mitral regurgitation in ARF is due to direct autoimmune attack on the valve, not solely papillary muscle dysfunction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This is ARF (autoimmune), not infective endocarditis (direct infection). There are no bacterial vegetations; the valve damage is from antibody cross-reactivity. The elevated ASO titers and clinical context confirm a post-streptococcal autoimmune process.

questions.topicName: Acute Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease
questions.stem: A 10-year-old girl presents with a 1-week history of impetigo on her lower extremities. Her mother is concerned about the child developing rheumatic fever since her older sibling had this condition. The girl's physical examination is otherwise unremarkable, and her vital signs are normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate response to the mother's concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ARF can follow any type of group A streptococcal infection, so close monitoring for cardiac symptoms is warranted

choices.label: B
choices.text: ARF follows GAS pharyngitis but not GAS skin infections, so impetigo does not increase her risk for ARF

choices.label: C
choices.text: ARF only develops in children under age 5, so this child is no longer in the at-risk age group for the condition

choices.label: D
choices.text: ARF requires at least three episodes of untreated strep infection before the autoimmune response can develop

choices.label: E
choices.text: ARF risk is determined entirely by genetic factors, and having a sibling with ARF confirms her genetic susceptibility

explanation.summary: ARF follows GAS pharyngitis but not GAS-associated skin infections such as impetigo. Therefore, this child's impetigo does not place her at increased risk for developing ARF.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material explicitly states that ARF follows GAS pharyngitis, but not GAS-associated skin infections (e.g., impetigo). While impetigo should be treated, it does not carry a risk of triggering the autoimmune cascade that leads to ARF.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This is incorrect because ARF specifically follows pharyngeal GAS infection, not all types of GAS infection. Skin infections like impetigo do not trigger the autoimmune process leading to ARF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ARF is a disease primarily affecting children (80% of cases), but it is not limited to those under age 5. Children of school age are commonly affected, and ARF occurs 1-5 weeks after streptococcal pharyngitis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no requirement for multiple prior infections before ARF can develop. Even a single untreated episode of GAS pharyngitis can trigger the autoimmune response, though untreated infections increase the risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While certain populations are at higher risk, ARF risk is primarily driven by environmental factors (poverty, overcrowding, limited healthcare access) and untreated GAS pharyngitis, not solely by genetic factors.

questions.topicName: Acute Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease
questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with a history of rheumatic heart disease presents with new-onset palpitations and an irregularly irregular pulse. Echocardiography shows severe mitral stenosis with a valve area of 1.0 cm² and significant left atrial dilation. She is started on anticoagulation therapy. Her physician explains that she is at risk for a serious complication related to her atrial pathology. Which of the following complications is the physician most likely concerned about?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aortic root dissection secondary to chronic pressure overload from mitral stenosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ventricular tachycardia from fibrotic involvement of the interventricular septum

choices.label: C
choices.text: Embolic stroke from thrombus formation in the dilated left atrium during atrial fibrillation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spontaneous pneumothorax from chronically elevated pulmonary venous pressures

choices.label: E
choices.text: Infective endocarditis from bacteremia seeding the calcified and stenotic mitral valve

explanation.summary: Chronic RHD with mitral stenosis leads to left atrial dilation and atrial fibrillation. The combination of atrial dilation and fibrillation promotes stasis of blood in the left atrium, creating conditions for thrombus formation, which can embolize to the brain causing stroke.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material identifies atrial fibrillation and embolic strokes as cardiac complications of chronic RHD. The irregularly irregular pulse confirms atrial fibrillation, and the left atrial dilation from mitral stenosis creates stasis that promotes thrombus formation. Anticoagulation was initiated specifically to prevent embolic stroke.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aortic root dissection is not a recognized complication of mitral stenosis or RHD. Aortic dissection is associated with conditions like Marfan syndrome, hypertension, and bicuspid aortic valve, not rheumatic mitral stenosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While heart block can occur in RHD from involvement of the conducting system, the irregularly irregular pulse indicates atrial fibrillation, not ventricular tachycardia. The concern here is embolic complications from atrial pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Spontaneous pneumothorax is not a complication of elevated pulmonary venous pressures from mitral stenosis. Pulmonary hypertension and pulmonary edema can develop, but not pneumothorax.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While damaged valves do increase IE risk, the physician's immediate concern given the atrial fibrillation and anticoagulation therapy is embolic stroke, which is a direct complication of the atrial fibrillation and left atrial stasis.

questions.topicName: Acute Rheumatic Fever and Rheumatic Heart Disease
questions.stem: An 11-year-old boy is diagnosed with acute rheumatic fever after presenting with migratory polyarthritis and carditis. He is started on prednisone and penicillin. His parents ask why he needs penicillin if the heart damage is caused by the immune system and not by bacteria directly infecting the heart. Which of the following is the most accurate explanation for the use of penicillin in this setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Penicillin suppresses the autoimmune response by reducing inflammatory mediator production in lymphocytes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Penicillin prevents secondary bacterial infection of the already-damaged heart valves during the acute phase

choices.label: C
choices.text: Penicillin eradicates any residual group A streptococcal organisms that may still be present in the patient

choices.label: D
choices.text: Penicillin acts synergistically with prednisone to reduce the severity of the type II hypersensitivity reaction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Penicillin reduces circulating M protein fragments that continue to stimulate cross-reactive antibody production

explanation.summary: In ARF treatment, penicillin is used to eradicate any residual GAS organisms. While the cardiac damage is autoimmune-mediated, eliminating residual bacteria prevents ongoing antigenic stimulation and reduces the risk of recurrence.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that ARF should be treated with prednisone (anti-inflammatory) as well as penicillin to eradicate any residual GAS organisms. The penicillin targets the bacteria, while prednisone addresses the inflammatory response.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Penicillin is a bactericidal antibiotic that targets bacterial cell wall synthesis. It has no direct immunosuppressive or anti-inflammatory properties. Prednisone serves the anti-inflammatory role in ARF treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The purpose of penicillin in ARF is to eradicate residual GAS, not to prevent secondary valve infection. The valve damage in ARF is autoimmune, not infectious, and infective endocarditis prophylaxis is a different clinical scenario.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Penicillin and prednisone do not act synergistically. They serve completely different purposes: penicillin eradicates bacteria while prednisone suppresses the inflammatory immune response.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While eliminating bacteria does reduce the source of M protein, the primary purpose of penicillin is to eradicate residual organisms, not specifically to reduce circulating M protein fragments. Antibodies against M protein persist long after the bacteria are eliminated.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with a history of intravenous drug use presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of fever, chills, and progressive dyspnea. On examination, his temperature is 39.2°C (102.6°F), and auscultation reveals a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases in intensity with deep inspiration. A chest radiograph shows multiple bilateral peripheral nodular opacities. Blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus. Which of the following best explains the pulmonary findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hematogenous spread of bacteria from a primary pulmonary focus to bilateral lung parenchyma

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immune complex deposition in the pulmonary vasculature causing diffuse alveolar hemorrhage

choices.label: C
choices.text: Septic emboli from tricuspid valve vegetations traveling through the pulmonary arteries to the lungs

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cardiogenic pulmonary edema from acute left ventricular failure due to aortic valve destruction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Aspiration pneumonia from altered mental status caused by septic emboli to the central nervous system

explanation.summary: This IV drug user has right-sided (tricuspid valve) endocarditis caused by S. aureus. The murmur increasing with inspiration (Carvallo sign) confirms tricuspid involvement. The bilateral pulmonary nodules represent septic emboli from tricuspid valve vegetations traveling through the pulmonary arteries to the lungs, causing septic pneumonia.
explanation.whyCorrect: In IV drug users, bacteria are injected into the venous system and lodge on the first valve encountered—the tricuspid valve. Vegetations on the tricuspid valve can embolize through the pulmonary arteries to the lungs, causing septic pulmonary infarcts that appear as multiple bilateral nodular opacities on chest radiograph.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The primary infection is on the tricuspid valve, not in the lungs. The pulmonary nodules result from septic emboli originating from the valve, not from hematogenous spread of a pulmonary infection.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While immune complex deposition can cause glomerulonephritis in IE, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage from immune complex deposition is not the mechanism for the discrete nodular opacities seen in this case. The pattern of multiple nodules suggests embolic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Left ventricular failure and cardiogenic pulmonary edema would present as bilateral interstitial or alveolar infiltrates, not discrete peripheral nodular opacities. Additionally, the tricuspid valve involvement would cause right-sided heart failure, not left ventricular failure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no mention of altered mental status, and the pattern of multiple bilateral peripheral nodules is characteristic of septic emboli, not aspiration pneumonia, which typically affects dependent lung segments.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with a 6-week history of low-grade fevers, night sweats, weight loss, and fatigue. He has a known history of mitral valve prolapse. On examination, his temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), and he has a new holosystolic murmur at the apex. Blood cultures drawn from three separate sites grow viridans group streptococci. Which of the following features of this organism best explains the indolent clinical course?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its ability to produce coagulase, allowing it to evade phagocytosis by forming protective fibrin barriers

choices.label: B
choices.text: Its capacity to form large, friable vegetations that rapidly destroy valve tissue and cause acute heart failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Its relatively low virulence compared to Staphylococcus aureus, producing smaller vegetations on damaged valves

choices.label: D
choices.text: Its intracellular survival within macrophages, making it resistant to antibiotic penetration and immune clearance

choices.label: E
choices.text: Its production of exotoxins that suppress the host immune response, preventing fever and systemic inflammation

explanation.summary: Viridans streptococci (including S. mutans and S. sanguinis) cause subacute IE characterized by an insidious onset because they are less virulent than S. aureus. They produce smaller vegetations on already-damaged or abnormal valves, leading to a more gradual clinical course.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that viridans streptococci are considerably less virulent than S. aureus and cause smaller vegetations on damaged or genetically abnormal valves. Their lower virulence explains the subacute presentation with insidious symptoms, as opposed to the rapid, destructive course of acute IE caused by S. aureus.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coagulase production is a characteristic of Staphylococcus aureus, not viridans streptococci. Coagulase-positive staphylococci use this enzyme to form fibrin clots around themselves as a defense mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Large, friable vegetations with rapid valve destruction describe acute IE caused by highly virulent organisms like S. aureus. Viridans streptococci cause smaller vegetations and less destructive disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Viridans streptococci are extracellular pathogens that do not characteristically survive intracellularly within macrophages. Their indolent course is due to low virulence, not immune evasion through intracellular survival.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Viridans streptococci do not produce exotoxins that suppress the immune response. Patients with subacute IE do develop fever, albeit low-grade, and other systemic symptoms—just more gradually than in acute IE.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a mechanical aortic valve replacement performed 3 weeks ago presents with fever and malaise. Blood cultures grow coagulase-negative staphylococci. Transesophageal echocardiography reveals a small vegetation on the prosthetic valve with no evidence of abscess. Which of the following best explains why this organism is the most likely pathogen in this clinical scenario?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The organism is part of the normal oral flora and was introduced during perioperative dental manipulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: The organism commonly contaminates water supplies used to prepare injectable drugs and solutions

choices.label: C
choices.text: The organism is a normal skin flora that can form biofilms on the smooth surface of prosthetic valves

choices.label: D
choices.text: The organism is associated with gastrointestinal translocation through ulcerated colonic mucosa

choices.label: E
choices.text: The organism produces toxins that specifically target prosthetic materials and accelerate device degradation

explanation.summary: S. epidermidis (coagulase-negative staphylococci) is the most common cause of early prosthetic valve endocarditis (within 60 days of surgery). As normal skin flora, it can be introduced during surgery and form biofilms on the smooth prosthetic surface.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that staphylococci most commonly colonize within 60 days of surgery, with S. epidermidis being the most common and S. aureus the second most common. The slick texture of prosthetic valves provides a good surface for biofilm formation—aggregates of bacteria surrounded by a sticky extracellular matrix.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oral flora organisms (viridans streptococci, HACEK organisms) are associated with dental procedures. Coagulase-negative staphylococci are normal skin flora, not oral flora, and are introduced through skin breakage during surgery.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Water contamination is associated with Pseudomonas aeruginosa, not coagulase-negative staphylococci. S. epidermidis is a skin commensal organism introduced through surgical or other skin-breaking procedures.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gastrointestinal translocation through ulcerated colonic mucosa is associated with Streptococcus gallolyticus (formerly S. bovis), which is linked to colon cancer, not coagulase-negative staphylococci.
whyWrongByChoice.E: S. epidermidis does not produce toxins that target prosthetic materials. Its pathogenicity in prosthetic valve endocarditis relates to its ability to form biofilms on smooth prosthetic surfaces, not through toxin production.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man is admitted with fever, fatigue, and weight loss for 3 weeks. He recently underwent a colonoscopy that revealed a large ulcerated mass in the ascending colon. Blood cultures from three separate sites grow gram-positive cocci in chains. Echocardiography reveals a 1.2 cm vegetation on the aortic valve. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enterococcus faecalis introduced from the genitourinary tract during transient bacteremia

choices.label: B
choices.text: Streptococcus gallolyticus translocated from the gastrointestinal tract through ulcerated colonic mucosa

choices.label: C
choices.text: Streptococcus mutans seeding the bloodstream during routine dental hygiene procedures

choices.label: D
choices.text: Staphylococcus aureus introduced through a break in the skin during recent surgical instrumentation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Streptococcus pneumoniae spreading hematogenously from a concurrent community-acquired pneumonia

explanation.summary: Streptococcus gallolyticus (formerly S. bovis) endocarditis is uniquely associated with colon cancer. The ulcerated colonic mass allows gut bacteria to enter the bloodstream, leading to bacteremia and subsequent valve infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that patients with colon cancer may have ulceration of the colon wall, allowing gut bacteria to enter the bloodstream. S. gallolyticus is a gut flora organism that is uniquely associated with colorectal cancer. The combination of gram-positive cocci in chains (consistent with streptococci) and a colon mass strongly suggests S. gallolyticus endocarditis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While Enterococcus can cause endocarditis, it is typically associated with genitourinary procedures and is not specifically linked to colon cancer. The presence of a colonic mass with ulceration strongly suggests S. gallolyticus.
whyWrongByChoice.C: S. mutans is a viridans streptococcus associated with dental procedures and subacute endocarditis on damaged valves. It is not associated with colon cancer or gastrointestinal translocation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: S. aureus is coagulase-positive and typically appears as gram-positive cocci in clusters, not chains. It causes acute endocarditis and is not specifically associated with colon cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.E: S. pneumoniae is a gram-positive diplococcus associated with pneumonia, meningitis, and otitis media. It is not a typical cause of endocarditis and is not associated with colorectal cancer.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a history of a bicuspid aortic valve presents with a 2-week history of low-grade fevers and fatigue. She had a dental cleaning 6 weeks ago and did not receive antibiotic prophylaxis. Physical examination reveals painless, erythematous, blanching lesions on her palms and a new diastolic murmur. Blood cultures are obtained. Which of the following physical findings, if also present, would be classified as an immunologic phenomenon according to the modified Duke criteria?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Splinter hemorrhages in the fingernail beds caused by microemboli to the nail-bed capillaries

choices.label: B
choices.text: Janeway lesions on the soles of the feet representing septic emboli to the dermis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Petechiae on the conjunctivae from small emboli lodging in the conjunctival blood vessels

choices.label: D
choices.text: Osler nodes on the fingertips representing immune-mediated vasculitis with localized inflammation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Septic pulmonary infarcts visible on chest radiograph as peripheral wedge-shaped opacities

explanation.summary: According to the modified Duke criteria, immunologic phenomena include glomerulonephritis, Osler nodes, Roth spots, and rheumatoid factor. Vascular lesions (a separate minor criterion) include arterial emboli, septic pulmonary infarcts, mycotic aneurysm, intracranial hemorrhage, conjunctival hemorrhages, and Janeway lesions.
explanation.whyCorrect: The modified Duke criteria specifically classify Osler nodes as an immunologic phenomenon, along with glomerulonephritis, Roth spots, and rheumatoid factor. Osler nodes are painful red lesions on the fingers or toes and represent immune complex-mediated vasculitis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Splinter hemorrhages are caused by microemboli and are a nonspecific finding. They are not specifically classified as either a vascular or immunologic criterion in the modified Duke criteria as listed in the source.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Janeway lesions are classified as a vascular lesion in the modified Duke criteria, not an immunologic phenomenon. They are painless erythematous lesions caused by septic emboli.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Conjunctival hemorrhages are classified as vascular lesions in the modified Duke criteria, not immunologic phenomena. They result from embolic vascular damage.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Septic pulmonary infarcts are classified as vascular lesions in the modified Duke criteria, representing embolic complications rather than immunologic phenomena.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset left-sided hemiplegia and expressive aphasia. CT angiography of the head reveals an embolic occlusion of the right middle cerebral artery. He has a temperature of 38.8°C (101.8°F) and a new diastolic murmur at the left sternal border. Transesophageal echocardiography shows a large mobile vegetation on the aortic valve. Blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus. Which of the following is the most common cause of death in patients with this condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Embolic stroke with hemorrhagic transformation leading to cerebral herniation and brainstem compression

choices.label: B
choices.text: Septic shock with multiorgan failure from uncontrolled bacteremia despite antibiotic therapy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heart failure from acute valvular insufficiency caused by vegetations and valvular abscesses

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mycotic aneurysm rupture in the cerebral vasculature causing massive subarachnoid hemorrhage

choices.label: E
choices.text: Renal failure from immune complex-mediated glomerulonephritis and progressive renal infarction

explanation.summary: Heart failure is the most common cause of death from infective endocarditis. It develops when vegetations and valvular abscesses cause acute valvular insufficiency, leading to hemodynamic compromise.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material explicitly states that heart failure is the most common cause of death from IE. Valvular abscesses can form from the vegetations, causing acute valvular insufficiency. In this patient with S. aureus acute IE, rapid valve destruction is characteristic, making heart failure the greatest mortality risk.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While embolic stroke is a serious complication of IE (as demonstrated in this patient), it is not the most common cause of death. Heart failure from valve destruction is more commonly fatal.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Sepsis can occur in IE but is not identified as the most common cause of death. Heart failure from acute valvular insufficiency is the leading cause of mortality.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mycotic aneurysm rupture is a recognized but relatively uncommon complication of IE. It is not the most common cause of death in IE patients.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glomerulonephritis from immune complex deposition is a recognized complication of IE but rarely causes death. Heart failure from valvular destruction is the most common fatal complication.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman with a history of IV drug use presents with fever, malaise, and a new systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration. Echocardiography reveals a large vegetation on the tricuspid valve with moderate tricuspid regurgitation. On physical examination, she has prominent jugular venous distension with a visible systolic pulsation in her neck veins. Which of the following best explains this jugular venous finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Superior vena cava obstruction from a septic thrombus extending from the tricuspid vegetation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Right ventricular contraction transmitted retrograde through an incompetent tricuspid valve into the right atrium and jugular veins

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increased left atrial pressure from concomitant mitral valve involvement causing pulmonary venous congestion

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pericardial effusion from extension of infection to the pericardium causing cardiac tamponade physiology

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bilateral internal jugular vein thrombosis from septic emboli originating from the tricuspid vegetation

explanation.summary: Tricuspid regurgitation from tricuspid valve endocarditis causes a bounding jugular venous pulse during systole. This occurs because the right ventricular contraction is transmitted retrograde through the incompetent tricuspid valve into the right atrium and jugular veins.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that tricuspid valve endocarditis leads to tricuspid regurgitation, which can cause a bounding jugular venous pulse during systole as the right ventricular contraction is transmitted into the right atrium and jugular veins. This is the classic 'cv wave' of tricuspid regurgitation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: SVC obstruction would cause constant, non-pulsatile jugular venous distension with facial plethora, not the systolic pulsation described. Septic thrombus extension to the SVC is extremely uncommon in tricuspid valve endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Left atrial pressure elevation from mitral valve involvement would cause pulmonary congestion and left-sided heart failure symptoms, but would not directly cause the systolic jugular venous pulsation seen with tricuspid regurgitation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cardiac tamponade would cause elevated, non-pulsatile jugular venous pressure with pulsus paradoxus and muffled heart sounds, not the systolic pulsation pattern described in this case.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bilateral jugular vein thrombosis would cause non-pulsatile jugular venous distension and would not produce the characteristic systolic pulsation. Septic emboli from the tricuspid valve travel to the lungs, not to the jugular veins.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man with a history of a prior episode of infective endocarditis is scheduled for a dental cleaning. His cardiologist recommends antibiotic prophylaxis before the procedure. Which of the following is the preferred prophylactic regimen for this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous vancomycin administered 2 hours prior to the procedure and continued for 48 hours after

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oral amoxicillin given as a single dose 30 to 60 minutes before the procedure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intramuscular penicillin G benzathine administered 1 week prior to the dental procedure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oral ciprofloxacin started 3 days before the procedure and continued for 7 days total

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone administered during the procedure and continued for 24 hours after

explanation.summary: For high-risk patients (including those with prior IE) undergoing dental procedures that manipulate gingival tissue or oral mucosa, the preferred prophylactic regimen is oral amoxicillin given as a single dose 30–60 minutes before the procedure.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material specifies that patients at high risk for IE (including those with prior endocarditis) undergoing dental procedures should receive antibiotic prophylaxis 30–60 minutes before the procedure, with oral amoxicillin as the preferred regimen given once.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IV vancomycin for 48 hours is excessive for dental prophylaxis. The recommended regimen is a single oral dose of amoxicillin, not an extended IV antibiotic course.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IM penicillin G benzathine one week prior is not the recommended prophylactic regimen for dental procedures. Prophylaxis should be given 30–60 minutes before the procedure, not a week in advance.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ciprofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone not recommended for IE prophylaxis. A 7-day course is excessive for prophylaxis, which requires only a single dose of amoxicillin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IV ceftriaxone during the procedure is not the preferred regimen. The standard recommendation is oral amoxicillin given 30–60 minutes before the procedure as a single dose.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with suspected infective endocarditis has three sets of blood cultures drawn from separate sites. After 5 days of incubation, all cultures remain negative. He has persistent fever, a new mitral regurgitation murmur, and a transesophageal echocardiogram showing a mitral valve vegetation. He recently returned from a trip to a rural area where he worked with livestock. Which of the following organisms should be specifically considered in this case of culture-negative endocarditis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Streptococcus mutans, a fastidious organism requiring extended culture incubation periods beyond 7 days

choices.label: B
choices.text: Staphylococcus epidermidis, which forms biofilms that prevent organisms from being released into the bloodstream

choices.label: C
choices.text: Coxiella burnetii, an obligate intracellular pathogen that does not grow in standard blood culture media

choices.label: D
choices.text: Pseudomonas aeruginosa, which requires selective culture media not included in standard blood culture bottles

choices.label: E
choices.text: Enterococcus faecalis, which is inhibited by the anticoagulants used in standard blood culture collection tubes

explanation.summary: When blood cultures are negative in a patient with strong clinical evidence of IE, culture-negative endocarditis should be considered. Coxiella burnetii is one of the unusual organisms associated with culture-negative endocarditis that requires serologic testing for detection.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that in culture-negative endocarditis, serologic tests can be done to detect unusual organisms like Coxiella, Chlamydia, or Bartonella. Coxiella burnetii (the agent of Q fever) is an obligate intracellular organism associated with livestock exposure and does not grow in standard blood culture media.

whyWrongByChoice.A: S. mutans is a viridans group streptococcus that grows readily in standard blood cultures within 48 hours. It is not associated with culture-negative endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While S. epidermidis can form biofilms, it still releases organisms into the bloodstream and can be detected by blood cultures. It is not a typical cause of culture-negative endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: P. aeruginosa grows well in standard blood culture media and does not require special selective media. It is not associated with culture-negative endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Enterococcus faecalis grows readily in standard blood culture bottles and is not inhibited by the collection tube additives. It is a common cause of subacute IE, not culture-negative endocarditis.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with no significant past medical history presents with fever, back pain, and a new-onset cardiac murmur. MRI of the lumbar spine reveals signal changes consistent with vertebral osteomyelitis at L4-L5 with adjacent discitis. Blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus. Echocardiography shows a large vegetation on the mitral valve. Which of the following best explains the relationship between his cardiac and spinal findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The vertebral infection preceded the endocarditis, with bacteria spreading hematogenously from the spine to the heart

choices.label: B
choices.text: Septic emboli from the mitral valve vegetation traveled through the arterial system to infect the vertebral bodies

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both findings represent separate, independent infections caused by the same organism at different anatomic sites

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immune complex deposition from the endocarditis caused an inflammatory reaction mimicking osteomyelitis on imaging

choices.label: E
choices.text: The spinal findings represent a metastatic tumor that eroded into blood vessels, causing bacteremia and subsequent endocarditis

explanation.summary: Vertebral osteomyelitis and discitis are recognized embolic complications of left-sided infective endocarditis. Vegetations on the mitral valve can embolize through the systemic arterial circulation to the vertebral bodies, causing septic arthritis and osteomyelitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material identifies osteomyelitis as a complication of IE resulting from embolization to the limbs and musculoskeletal system. Patients with vertebral involvement present with deep bone pain and back pain. The mitral valve vegetation serves as the embolic source, with septic emboli traveling through the arterial circulation to the vertebral bodies.

whyWrongByChoice.A: In this clinical scenario, the endocarditis is the primary source with secondary embolic osteomyelitis, not the reverse. The large mitral valve vegetation is the embolic source causing the vertebral infection.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The temporal association and the presence of the same organism at both sites, combined with a large valve vegetation capable of embolization, strongly suggest a causal relationship rather than two independent infections.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Immune complex deposition in IE causes glomerulonephritis, not vertebral osteomyelitis. The MRI findings of signal changes with discitis indicate a true infection, not an inflammatory mimicker.
whyWrongByChoice.E: There is no evidence of a spinal tumor. The findings are consistent with septic osteomyelitis from embolic spread of the endocarditis infection, which is a well-recognized complication of IE.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with a mechanical mitral valve placed 4 months ago presents with low-grade fever and fatigue for 2 weeks. Blood cultures grow Streptococcus sanguinis. Which of the following best explains why a streptococcal species rather than a staphylococcal species is the most likely pathogen in this timeframe?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Streptococci are more virulent than staphylococci and preferentially colonize prosthetic materials over native tissue

choices.label: B
choices.text: Streptococci most commonly colonize prosthetic valves more than 60 days after surgery, while staphylococci predominate within the first 60 days

choices.label: C
choices.text: Streptococci form thicker biofilms on prosthetic valves than staphylococci, giving them a colonization advantage at all timepoints

choices.label: D
choices.text: Staphylococci are eliminated by the standard perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis, leaving streptococci as the predominant pathogens

choices.label: E
choices.text: Streptococci enter the bloodstream more frequently than staphylococci due to their ubiquitous presence in the oral cavity

explanation.summary: In prosthetic valve endocarditis, the timing of infection determines the most likely pathogen. Staphylococci (especially S. epidermidis) most commonly colonize within 60 days of surgery, while streptococci most commonly colonize more than 60 days after surgery.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material explicitly states that staphylococci most commonly colonize within 60 days of surgery (with S. epidermidis being the most common), while streptococci most commonly colonize more than 60 days after surgery. Since this patient is 4 months (approximately 120 days) post-surgery, streptococci are the expected pathogens.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Streptococci are generally less virulent than staphylococci, particularly S. aureus. They do not preferentially colonize prosthetic materials; staphylococci are actually more associated with biofilm formation on prosthetics.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Staphylococci, particularly S. epidermidis, are known for forming robust biofilms on prosthetic surfaces. The timing difference is not related to biofilm thickness but to the different routes and timing of bacterial introduction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis does not selectively eliminate staphylococci while sparing streptococci. The temporal pattern relates to the natural history of prosthetic valve colonization from different sources.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While streptococci are present in the oral cavity, this does not explain the temporal pattern. Staphylococci are ubiquitous on the skin and enter the bloodstream frequently as well. The key factor is the timing of colonization relative to surgery.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 10-day history of high fevers reaching 40°C, rigors, and progressive dyspnea. He has no significant medical history and no history of drug use. On examination, he appears acutely ill, with a blood pressure of 88/40 mmHg, heart rate of 124 bpm, and a loud diastolic murmur at the left sternal border. Blood cultures grow gram-positive cocci in clusters. Which of the following statements best characterizes his expected clinical course?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most patients with this presentation recover fully with standard 2-week oral antibiotic therapy alone

choices.label: B
choices.text: The mortality rate is approximately 50% despite appropriate treatment due to rapid valve destruction

choices.label: C
choices.text: The clinical course is typically indolent, with gradual worsening of symptoms over several months

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surgical valve replacement is rarely needed since antibiotics alone are sufficient for native valve disease

choices.label: E
choices.text: Prognosis is excellent if blood cultures are obtained and targeted antibiotic therapy is initiated promptly

explanation.summary: This patient has acute infective endocarditis caused by S. aureus (gram-positive cocci in clusters) on a native valve. Acute IE caused by S. aureus has a mortality rate of 50% despite treatment due to rapid valve destruction and its high virulence.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that S. aureus causes acute IE with large vegetations and rapid valve destruction. Symptoms present rapidly, and the mortality rate is 50% despite treatment. The patient's acute presentation with hemodynamic instability (hypotension) and acute heart failure is characteristic of this aggressive disease course.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IE requires 4 to 6 weeks of IV antibiotic therapy, not 2 weeks of oral antibiotics. Additionally, acute S. aureus endocarditis has high mortality even with appropriate treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An indolent course with gradual worsening describes subacute IE caused by less virulent organisms like viridans streptococci. Acute IE caused by S. aureus presents rapidly with fulminant symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Surgical valve replacement may be required in IE that progresses despite antibiotic therapy. Given the 50% mortality rate and rapid valve destruction in acute S. aureus IE, surgery is frequently necessary.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Despite prompt targeted therapy, the mortality rate for acute S. aureus native valve endocarditis remains approximately 50% due to the organism's high virulence and rapid valve destruction.

questions.stem: A 41-year-old woman with a prosthetic aortic valve and persistent fevers undergoes fundoscopic examination as part of her workup for suspected infective endocarditis. The ophthalmologist identifies retinal hemorrhages with small white or pale centers surrounded by red, flame-shaped areas of hemorrhage. Which of the following is the correct term for this ophthalmologic finding, and in which category does it fall under the modified Duke criteria?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cotton-wool spots; classified as a major criterion indicating end-organ damage from septic embolization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Roth spots; classified as a minor criterion under immunologic phenomena along with glomerulonephritis and Osler nodes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hollenhorst plaques; classified as a minor criterion under vascular lesions along with Janeway lesions and mycotic aneurysms

choices.label: D
choices.text: Papilledema; classified as a minor criterion indicating elevated intracranial pressure from cerebral embolic complications

choices.label: E
choices.text: Roth spots; classified as a major criterion representing definitive evidence of endocardial vegetation embolization

explanation.summary: Roth spots are retinal hemorrhages with a white or pale center surrounded by red, flame-shaped hemorrhage. In the modified Duke criteria, they are classified as a minor criterion under immunologic phenomena, alongside glomerulonephritis, Osler nodes, and rheumatoid factor.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material describes Roth spots as retinal hemorrhages with a white center and states they are classified under immunologic phenomena in the modified Duke criteria, along with glomerulonephritis, Osler nodes, and rheumatoid factor.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cotton-wool spots are retinal nerve fiber layer infarcts that appear as white, fluffy lesions without the surrounding flame-shaped hemorrhage characteristic of Roth spots. Roth spots have a distinctive white center within a hemorrhage.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hollenhorst plaques are cholesterol emboli visible in retinal arterioles, associated with carotid artery atherosclerosis, not IE. The described finding with white centers in flame-shaped hemorrhages is a Roth spot, not a Hollenhorst plaque.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Papilledema is optic disc swelling from elevated intracranial pressure and has a different appearance from Roth spots. The finding described—retinal hemorrhages with pale centers—is specific to Roth spots.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While Roth spots are correctly identified, they are a minor criterion under immunologic phenomena, not a major criterion. The major Duke criteria include positive blood cultures, imaging evidence of vegetations, new valvular regurgitation, and direct surgical inspection.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old man with a history of IV heroin use is diagnosed with infective endocarditis caused by Staphylococcus aureus involving the tricuspid valve. He is started on IV nafcillin based on antibiotic susceptibility results. After 3 weeks of therapy, he continues to have persistent fevers and blood cultures remain positive. Repeat echocardiography shows enlarging vegetations with a new paravalvular abscess. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Add gentamicin to the nafcillin regimen for synergistic bactericidal activity and continue medical therapy for 6 more weeks

choices.label: B
choices.text: Switch to vancomycin empirically in case of methicillin resistance despite initial susceptibility testing showing sensitivity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Proceed with surgical valve replacement given progression of disease despite appropriate antibiotic therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discontinue all antibiotics for 72 hours to allow immune reconstitution before restarting with a different antibiotic class

choices.label: E
choices.text: Obtain a PET scan to confirm the abscess before making any changes to the current antibiotic regimen

explanation.summary: When IE progresses despite appropriate antibiotic therapy, with persistent bacteremia and development of paravalvular abscess, surgical valve replacement is indicated. The source material states that IE that progresses despite antibiotic therapy may require surgical valve replacement.
explanation.whyCorrect: The source material states that IE that progresses despite antibiotic therapy may require surgical valve replacement. This patient has persistent positive blood cultures, ongoing fevers, enlarging vegetations, and a new paravalvular abscess after 3 weeks of appropriate therapy—all indications for surgical intervention.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Simply adding gentamicin without addressing the surgical need is insufficient. The patient has failed medical therapy with development of a paravalvular abscess, which is a clear surgical indication.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The organism was already found to be methicillin-sensitive, making nafcillin the appropriate targeted therapy. Switching to vancomycin would provide inferior coverage for MSSA and does not address the mechanical problem of the abscess.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Discontinuing antibiotics in a patient with active endocarditis and persistent bacteremia would be extremely dangerous and could lead to sepsis, further embolic events, and death.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The paravalvular abscess has already been identified on echocardiography. Delaying surgical intervention to obtain additional imaging is inappropriate when the patient has clear indications for surgery based on treatment failure.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with a history of metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma presents to the emergency department with acute-onset left-sided weakness and slurred speech. Her temperature is 37.1°C (98.8°F), blood pressure is 138/82 mmHg, heart rate is 92/min, and respiratory rate is 18/min. Cardiac auscultation reveals a new holosystolic murmur at the apex. CT of the head shows an acute ischemic infarct in the right middle cerebral artery territory. Three sets of blood cultures drawn over 24 hours show no growth. Echocardiography reveals small, bland-appearing vegetations on the mitral valve without valvular destruction. Which of the following best explains the pathogenesis of the valvular lesions in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoantibody-mediated attack on phospholipid-containing cell membranes leading to excess clot formation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deposition of immune complexes and cellular debris on the valve surface from systemic autoimmune disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct seeding of the valve by circulating organisms from a distant infectious focus during bacteremia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hypercoagulable state with production of procoagulant substances and cytokine-mediated endothelial damage

choices.label: E
choices.text: Molecular mimicry between streptococcal antigens and cardiac valve tissue triggering autoimmune valvulitis

explanation.summary: This patient with metastatic pancreatic cancer has nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis (NBTE), which arises from a hypercoagulable state caused by malignancy. Cancer cells produce procoagulant substances and cytokines that damage the endothelium, leading to sterile vegetation formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Malignancy is associated with NBTE due to activation of the coagulation system. Cancer cells produce procoagulant substances and cytokines that damage the endothelium, promoting sterile thrombus formation on heart valves. The negative blood cultures and bland, nondestructive vegetations confirm NBTE rather than infective endocarditis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Autoantibody-mediated attack on phospholipid-containing membranes describes antiphospholipid syndrome, which can cause NBTE but is not the mechanism in a patient whose underlying condition is metastatic cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Immune complex deposition describes the mechanism seen in systemic lupus erythematosus-associated NBTE (Libman-Sacks endocarditis), not malignancy-associated NBTE.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Direct seeding by circulating organisms describes infective endocarditis, which is excluded by the negative blood cultures and the bland, nondestructive nature of the vegetations.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Molecular mimicry between streptococcal antigens and valve tissue describes the pathogenesis of rheumatic heart disease, not NBTE.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is brought to the hospital after experiencing sudden-onset right arm weakness and difficulty speaking. MRI of the brain confirms an acute ischemic stroke. Transesophageal echocardiography reveals vegetations present on both the atrial and ventricular surfaces of the mitral valve leaflets. Blood cultures are negative. Which of the following best characterizes the vegetations found on echocardiography?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Large, irregular, destructive growths composed of fibrin, bacteria, and inflammatory cells on the valve surface

choices.label: B
choices.text: Small to medium-sized vegetations that can be attached to either side of the valve leaflets

choices.label: C
choices.text: Firm, fibrotic nodules along the valve line of closure resulting from prior episodes of rheumatic carditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Tiny, flat, nondestructive deposits of fibrin and platelets located exclusively on the atrial surface of the valve

choices.label: E
choices.text: Calcified, stenotic lesions causing valve commissural fusion with accompanying chordal thickening and shortening

explanation.summary: This patient with SLE has Libman-Sacks endocarditis, a form of NBTE. Libman-Sacks endocarditis is characterized by small to medium-sized vegetations that can be attached to either side of the valve leaflets, distinguishing it from other forms of endocarditis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Libman-Sacks endocarditis (the sterile endocarditis of SLE) is characterized by small to medium-sized vegetations that can be attached to either side of the valve leaflets. The presence of vegetations on both the atrial and ventricular surfaces of the mitral valve is a hallmark distinguishing feature of this condition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Large, irregular, destructive vegetations composed of fibrin, bacteria, and inflammatory cells describe infective endocarditis, which is excluded by the negative blood cultures and the bilateral valve surface involvement.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Firm, fibrotic nodules along the line of closure resulting from prior rheumatic carditis describe chronic rheumatic heart disease, not the acute vegetations of Libman-Sacks endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Small, flat deposits exclusively on the atrial surface is an incomplete description; Libman-Sacks vegetations characteristically appear on both sides of the valve leaflets, not just one surface.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Calcified, stenotic lesions with commissural fusion and chordal changes describe chronic rheumatic mitral stenosis, not Libman-Sacks endocarditis.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old man with recently diagnosed advanced gastric carcinoma presents with acute left flank pain and hematuria. His temperature is 36.9°C (98.4°F), blood pressure is 130/78 mmHg, and heart rate is 88/min. CT abdomen shows a wedge-shaped area of hypodensity in the left kidney consistent with renal infarction. Echocardiography reveals small, bland vegetations on the aortic valve. Three sets of blood cultures are negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacologic treatment for this patient's cardiac condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Empiric broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics targeting common endocarditis pathogens for six weeks

choices.label: B
choices.text: High-dose intravenous corticosteroids to suppress the underlying inflammatory autoimmune process

choices.label: C
choices.text: Systemic anticoagulation with heparin followed by transition to warfarin to prevent further embolization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and clopidogrel to inhibit thrombus formation on the valve surface

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous thrombolytic therapy with tissue plasminogen activator to dissolve the valvular vegetations

explanation.summary: This patient with advanced gastric carcinoma and sterile vegetations on echocardiography has NBTE. Management involves treating the underlying cause and anticoagulation with heparin (unfractionated or low molecular weight), then transitioning to warfarin (coumadin) to prevent emboli.
explanation.whyCorrect: Treatment of NBTE involves identifying and treating the underlying cause (in this case, malignancy) and anticoagulation with heparin (unfractionated or low molecular weight) followed by warfarin to prevent embolic complications, unless contraindicated.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Empiric antibiotics are used for infective endocarditis, which is excluded in this patient by the negative blood cultures and bland, nondestructive sterile vegetations characteristic of NBTE.
whyWrongByChoice.B: High-dose corticosteroids would be appropriate if the underlying cause were SLE, but this patient's NBTE is due to malignancy, and corticosteroids do not address the hypercoagulable state driving vegetation formation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Antiplatelet therapy alone is insufficient for NBTE; the recommended treatment is systemic anticoagulation with heparin followed by warfarin to prevent the thrombotic complications.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thrombolytic therapy is not indicated for NBTE vegetations; it carries a high bleeding risk and does not address the underlying hypercoagulable state that drives vegetation formation.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with a history of recurrent deep vein thromboses and two prior miscarriages presents with acute-onset visual field deficits. Laboratory studies reveal a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time that does not correct with a mixing study, and positive anticardiolipin and anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I antibodies. Echocardiography shows small vegetations on the mitral valve. Blood cultures are negative. Which of the following conditions most directly predisposed this patient to the formation of these vegetations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antiphospholipid syndrome causing autoantibody-mediated attack on cell membranes and excess clot formation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Disseminated intravascular coagulation triggered by an occult infectious process causing consumptive coagulopathy

choices.label: C
choices.text: Factor V Leiden mutation causing resistance to activated protein C and increased venous thromboembolism risk

choices.label: D
choices.text: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia causing paradoxical platelet activation and widespread arterial thrombosis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Protein C deficiency causing impaired regulation of the coagulation cascade and recurrent thrombotic episodes

explanation.summary: This patient has antiphospholipid syndrome (APS), characterized by recurrent thromboses, pregnancy losses, and antiphospholipid antibodies. APS is one of the three major conditions predisposing to NBTE, alongside malignancy and SLE.
explanation.whyCorrect: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) occurs when autoantibodies attack cell membranes, causing excess clot formation. APS is a well-established cause of NBTE. This patient's history of recurrent DVTs, miscarriages, and positive antiphospholipid antibodies with sterile valvular vegetations is classic for APS-associated NBTE.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Disseminated intravascular coagulation from an infectious process would present with consumptive coagulopathy and does not specifically cause sterile valvular vegetations; additionally, the clinical history is classic for APS.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Factor V Leiden causes hereditary thrombophilia with venous thromboembolism risk but is not a recognized cause of NBTE and does not explain the antiphospholipid antibodies or pregnancy losses.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia requires prior heparin exposure and presents with thrombocytopenia and thrombosis; it is not associated with sterile valvular vegetations or positive antiphospholipid antibodies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Protein C deficiency causes a hypercoagulable state with recurrent thrombosis but is not a recognized predisposing condition for NBTE and does not explain the positive antiphospholipid antibodies.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with stage IV lung adenocarcinoma dies in the hospital. At autopsy, small, smooth vegetations are found along the line of closure of the mitral valve. Microscopic examination reveals bland fibrin and platelet deposits with no evidence of bacteria, fungi, or inflammatory infiltrate. The valve leaflets are intact without destruction. This finding was not identified during the patient's lifetime. Which of the following best explains why this condition was not diagnosed antemortem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Patients are often asymptomatic because the vegetations are nondestructive and rarely impair valve function

choices.label: B
choices.text: Echocardiography is unable to detect vegetations smaller than 2 cm in diameter on cardiac valves

choices.label: C
choices.text: Blood cultures are typically negative, making it impossible to differentiate this condition from infective endocarditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: The vegetations exclusively affect the right-sided valves, which are difficult to assess on standard imaging

choices.label: E
choices.text: The vegetations rapidly resolve with anticoagulation therapy, leaving no evidence on subsequent imaging

explanation.summary: NBTE is most often diagnosed at autopsy because patients are frequently asymptomatic. The sterile vegetations are nondestructive and rarely impair valve function, so there may be no clinical indication to perform echocardiography.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with NBTE are often asymptomatic because the valvular lesions are non-infectious and rarely result in destruction of the valve, unlike infective endocarditis. Since there are often no signs of valvular or heart dysfunction, the condition frequently goes undiagnosed during life and is discovered as an incidental finding at autopsy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Echocardiography can detect vegetations much smaller than 2 cm and is the best test for detecting valvular vegetations; the issue is that patients are often asymptomatic and the test is never ordered.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While blood cultures are negative in NBTE, echocardiography can detect the vegetations if performed; the issue is that patients lack symptoms prompting the workup, not an inability to differentiate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: NBTE vegetations can occur on any valve, and those associated with SLE favor the mitral and aortic valves; they do not exclusively affect right-sided valves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vegetations in NBTE do not rapidly resolve with anticoagulation; anticoagulation is used to prevent embolization, not to dissolve existing vegetations.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) presents with sudden-onset confusion and left-sided hemiparesis. Echocardiography reveals vegetations on the mitral valve. Blood cultures are negative. Laboratory studies show positive ANA, anti-dsDNA, and anti-Smith antibodies. Which of the following long-term valvular complications is most characteristic of this patient's cardiac condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Aortic root dilation with annuloaortic ectasia requiring emergent aortic root replacement surgery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bacterial superinfection of the sterile vegetations progressing to fulminant septic endocarditis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Progressive valve destruction with leaflet perforation and flail segments requiring valve replacement

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transmural myocardial abscess formation with extension into the interventricular septum and conduction system

choices.label: E
choices.text: Fibrosis and scarring of the healed lesions producing changes resembling chronic rheumatic heart disease

explanation.summary: Libman-Sacks endocarditis (sterile endocarditis associated with SLE) characteristically heals with scarring. The ensuing fibrosis can result in lesions that resemble chronic rheumatic heart disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: The lesions of Libman-Sacks endocarditis characteristically heal with scarring, and the ensuing fibrosis can result in lesions that resemble chronic rheumatic heart disease. This is a distinctive long-term complication specific to SLE-associated NBTE.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aortic root dilation with annuloaortic ectasia is associated with connective tissue disorders like Marfan syndrome, not with the valvular lesions of Libman-Sacks endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bacterial superinfection of sterile vegetations is not a characteristic complication of Libman-Sacks endocarditis; the vegetations remain sterile.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Progressive valve destruction with leaflet perforation is characteristic of infective endocarditis; sterile vegetations of NBTE are rarely destructive to the valve.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Myocardial abscess formation with septal extension is a complication of invasive infective endocarditis, not the sterile, nondestructive vegetations seen in Libman-Sacks endocarditis.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with metastatic colon cancer presents with acute left upper quadrant abdominal pain. CT scan reveals a splenic infarction.
